title: Noise-like pulse laser source with ultrabroadband tunability and coherence-limited sub-structure
abstract: High brightness and low coherence laser sources with wideband tunability are essential for many full-field imaging applications aiming for high contrast and speckle free performance. However, this combination of parameters is challenging to achieve. The current solutions focus on decreasing spatial coherence or generation of time-varying speckle patterns, while suppression of temporal coherence typically compromises brightness. Here we demonstrate a wideband pulsed laser source with low temporal coherence and the absence of phase correlation between pulses as an alternative approach with simultaneous time and frequency diversity. The full gain spectrum of a Tm doped fiber laser (1650 nm 2000 nm) is operated in a tunable noise like pulse regime, which by nature is composed of countless structured elementary events with uncorrelated phases randomly varying from bunch to bunch. The measured spectral widths range from 13.8 nm to 18.8 nm, while the average output power varies between 63.3 mW and 213 mW. Numerical simulations reveal that temporal coherence decreases significantly with increasing optical gain, dropping from near unity at low gain to approximately 0.2 at high gain. The startup dynamics of the noise like pulse laser are experimentally studied using the dispersive Fourier transformation (DFT) method. Based on single shot spectra and frequency resolved optical gating traces, the coherence properties of the laser are further analyzed by calculating the mutual coherence function and cross-spectral density. The noise like pulse laser exhibits a coherence time of approximately 100 fs and an average pulse burst duration of about 40 ps in the high-gain regime.
one_liner: A tunable Tm-fiber noise-like pulse laser delivers bright, ultrabroadband output with very low temporal coherence.
why_it_matters: It offers a rare combination of high brightness, broad tunability, and speckle-suppressing low coherence for demanding optical imaging systems.
detailed_summary: The authors operate a Tm-doped fiber laser across 1650-2000 nm in a noise-like pulse regime whose sub-pulses have random, uncorrelated phases from burst to burst. They measure 13.8-18.8 nm spectral widths and 63.3-213 mW average power, while simulations show temporal coherence falling from near 1 to about 0.2 with increasing gain. Single-shot DFT spectra, FROG traces, and coherence-function analysis indicate about 100 fs coherence time and about 40 ps average burst duration in the high-gain regime.
main_result: They demonstrate a widely tunable 1.65-2.0 um pulsed laser source with high power, broadband spectra, and strongly reduced temporal coherence.
application_angle: The source is well suited to full-field imaging and other optical systems needing bright illumination with reduced speckle and temporal interference artifacts.

title: Perfect state transfer in quantum photonic networks based on Fourier modes
abstract: We propose a quantum network consisting of optical waveguides in the linear regime for quantum state transfer. The circular topology of our network introduces novel functionalities that enable us to analytically identify the conditions under which perfect state transfer (PST) is achievable. We utilize the properties of the Fourier modes, in particular the zero Fourier modes, which provide a protected subspace for the efficient propagation of quantum states, resulting in PST to the diametrically opposite site in a network with any number of sites $N = 4n$. The coupling profiles in the photonic network modulate the number of zero-energy eigenmodes, with uniform couplings yielding more than evanescent ones, confirming that the expedition of observed PST originates from the collapse of the eigenvalue spectrum into three distinct eigenvalue blocks, including $N/2$ manifolds of zero Fourier modes. We investigate PST in both discrete- and continuous-variable input regimes, using single photon state, Schrödinger cat states, and two-mode squeezed vacuum state. Our findings apply to the engineering of quantum networks and photonic lattices, paving the way for applications in controlled routing in integrated quantum circuits.
one_liner: A circular waveguide network achieves analytically predictable perfect state transfer using protected zero Fourier modes.
why_it_matters: It gives an integrated-photonics route to robust state routing for both discrete- and continuous-variable quantum communication.
detailed_summary: The paper studies linear optical waveguide networks with circular topology and shows that for any site number N = 4n, zero Fourier modes form a protected subspace enabling perfect transfer to the diametrically opposite node. The mechanism is tied to spectral collapse into three eigenvalue blocks, including N/2 zero-mode manifolds, and depends on the coupling profile. The analysis covers single-photon, Schrodinger-cat, and two-mode squeezed vacuum inputs, extending the protocol beyond qubit encodings.
main_result: They derive exact conditions under which perfect state transfer occurs in circular photonic networks for arbitrary N = 4n.
application_angle: This can inform on-chip quantum routers and interconnects that need deterministic state delivery across photonic lattices or network nodes.

title: Sparse Spectral Imaging for Thickness Mapping of 3R-MoS$_2$ on PDMS
abstract: We present a non-destructive, spatially resolved thickness characterization method for rhombohedral (3R) molybdenum disulfide (MoS$_2$) on polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) substrates. Unlike broadband spectroscopic approaches, the proposed method reduces the measurement to a small number of discrete intensity images, enabling direct thickness mapping with a conventional microscope architecture and commercially available bandpass filters. Our approach combines a systematic framework for selecting optimal discrete wavelength samples of the material's reflectance with a robust thickness retrieval algorithm based on a multivariate Gaussian probability model. By sampling the reflectance with just five strategically chosen near-infrared bandpass filters, we demonstrate thickness characterization up to 691 nm with a mean 95% confidence-interval width of 8.3 nm. The method is adaptable to other van der Waals materials and conventional optical thin-film systems. It therefore provides a foundation for scalable, real-time thickness characterization in, e.g., dry-transfer fabrication workflows, where thickness screening remains a critical bottleneck for the production of van der Waals heterostructure devices.
one_liner: Five-band sparse spectral imaging maps 3R-MoS2 thickness on PDMS with nanometer-scale confidence bounds.
why_it_matters: It turns a fabrication bottleneck into a fast, microscope-compatible screening method for van der Waals device workflows.
detailed_summary: The method replaces broadband spectroscopy with a small set of discrete reflectance measurements chosen by an optimization framework. Using five near-infrared bandpass filters and a multivariate Gaussian retrieval model, the authors characterize 3R-MoS2 thicknesses up to 691 nm with mean 95% confidence-interval width of 8.3 nm. Because it runs on conventional microscope hardware, it is compatible with spatially resolved, non-destructive process monitoring.
main_result: They demonstrate accurate spatial thickness retrieval for 3R-MoS2 using only five strategically selected spectral images.
application_angle: The approach is practical for real-time flake screening and thickness control during dry-transfer assembly of van der Waals heterostructures.

title: Diamond membranes: platform for photonic and opto-mechanical applications
abstract: Diamond 1 - 10 micrometers thick membranes are platform for photonic, quantum and opto-mechanic devices with applications across UV-IR spectral ranges. IR characterization of diamond gratings in reflection and transmission showed a change of the IR absorbance dichroism between positive and negative when the grating period was 1-2 wavelengths (free space) including inside the region of the intrinsic diamond absorbance. Femtosecond laser cutting of micrometers-wide and mm-long structures are demonstrated by steps of carbonization > 0.4 J/cm2/pulse (1030 nm/200 fs) and oxidation of diamond membranes. Light intensity distribution inside form-birefringent diamond structure was modeled for a scaled-down structure and wavelength to reveal characteristic interference patterns for different polarizations.
one_liner: Micron-scale diamond membranes are characterized and laser-processed as photonic and optomechanical device platforms.
why_it_matters: Diamond combines wide spectral transparency and mechanical robustness, making membrane processing valuable for integrated quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: The work examines 1-10 um diamond membranes as a platform spanning photonic, quantum, and optomechanical use cases. IR reflection and transmission measurements on diamond gratings show a sign change in absorbance dichroism when the grating period is on the order of 1-2 free-space wavelengths, including inside intrinsic diamond absorption bands. The authors also demonstrate femtosecond-laser cutting via carbonization and oxidation pathways and model polarization-dependent field distributions in form-birefringent structures.
main_result: They show optical anisotropy control and femtosecond microfabrication of thin diamond membrane structures relevant to photonic device engineering.
application_angle: These results support manufacturable diamond-based components for integrated photonics, quantum emitters, and optomechanical architectures across UV to IR wavelengths.

title: Measurement-Adapted Eigentask Representations for Photon-Limited Optical Readout
abstract: Optical readout in low-light imaging is fundamentally limited by measurement noise, including photon shot noise, detector noise, and quantization error. In this regime, downstream inference depends not only on the optical front end, but also on how noisy high-dimensional sensor measurements are represented before classification or decision-making. Here we show that eigentasks provide a measurement-adapted representation for optical sensor outputs by ordering readout features according to their resolvability under noise. Using experimental data from a lens-based optical imaging system and a reanalysis of published data from a single-photon-detection neural network, we find that eigentask representations frequently outperform standard baselines including principal component analysis and filtering-based compression. The advantage is most pronounced in photon-limited, few-shot, and higher-difficulty classification regimes. In few-shot MPEG-7 classification, for example, the advantage over other methods reaches about 10 percentage points as the number of classes increases. In these settings, eigentasks yield more informative low-dimensional features and improve sample-efficient downstream learning. These results identify measurement-adapted representation as a promising strategy for optical inference when photon budget, acquisition time, and task complexity are constrained.
one_liner: Measurement-adapted eigentasks improve optical inference from noisy, photon-limited sensor readout.
why_it_matters: It shows representation design itself can materially improve low-light optical classification without changing the physical sensor.
detailed_summary: The authors propose eigentask representations that rank sensor features by how resolvable they are under realistic measurement noise, including shot noise, detector noise, and quantization. On experimental lens-based imaging data and reanalyzed single-photon-detection neural-network data, these representations often outperform PCA and filtering-based compression. Gains are strongest in photon-starved, few-shot, and difficult classification settings, reaching about 10 percentage points on few-shot MPEG-7 tasks as class count rises.
platform_or_system: low-light optical readout and single-photon detection pipelines
main_result: They demonstrate that noise-aware eigentask features yield more informative low-dimensional optical measurements than standard compression baselines.
application_angle: The method can improve sample efficiency and decision quality in low-light imaging, optical sensing, and constrained acquisition systems.

title: A Single-Molecule Spin-Photon Interface
abstract: Optical interfaces that connect long-lived spin qubits to photons are a central requirement for quantum networking and distributed quantum information processing. Currently, solid-state atomic defects are leading candidates due to their inherent spin and optical coherence. Building on these advancements, synthetically tailored molecular systems represent a fundamental change in the field, utilizing precise atomic control and consistent bottom-up assembly. However, the lack of a robust spin-photon interface combining bright fluorescence, high spectral stability, and the persistent spin lifetimes inherent to ground-state systems has prohibited the detection of individual molecular qubits. Here we show that a triplet ground state carbene molecule, embedded within a structurally matched host crystal, functions as a robust spin-photon interface with single-molecule addressability. The system exhibits narrow zero-phonon lines, spectral stability over more than an hour, spin-selective optical transitions and single-molecule optically detected magnetic resonance. Coherent control yields millisecond-scale dynamical-decoupling coherence and tens-of-milliseconds spin relaxation at a temperature of 4.5 K. These results establish molecular qubits as a viable platform for single-emitter quantum optics while preserving the advantages of bottom-up chemical design and processable materials.
one_liner: A single carbene molecule in a matched crystal acts as a stable spin-photon interface with optical and spin coherence.
why_it_matters: It establishes chemically engineered molecules as credible single-emitter nodes for quantum networks and nanoscale quantum sensing.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates single-molecule addressability of a triplet-ground-state carbene embedded in a structurally matched host crystal at 4.5 K. The system shows narrow zero-phonon lines, hour-scale spectral stability, spin-selective optical transitions, and single-molecule optically detected magnetic resonance. Coherent control achieves millisecond-scale dynamical-decoupling coherence and spin relaxation times of tens of milliseconds, combining optical accessibility with long-lived ground-state spin physics.
platform_or_system: single carbene molecule in host crystal
main_result: They realize a robust single-molecule spin-photon interface with stable optical emission, ODMR, and long spin coherence and relaxation times.
application_angle: This opens a path to bottom-up quantum network nodes and molecular quantum sensors that can be chemically tailored and integrated into processable materials.

title: Bulk-Edge Correspondence via Higher Gauge Theory
abstract: More profound than bulk topological order of quantum materials is only its unwinding via gapless excitations along boundaries of the sample. We recast this bulk-edge correspondence -- for the experimentally relevant case of fractional quantum Hall (FQH) systems -- in terms of effective relative higher gauge theory, controlled by choices of classifying fibrations. For FQH systems, we identify the complex Hopf fibration as classifying the bulk/boundary topological effects, and find that it yields a non-Lagrangian reconstruction of Floreanini-Jackiw/Wess-Zumino-Witten chiral edge currents. Remarkably, the resulting effective FQH higher gauge theory turns out to be "geometrically engineered" on M2/M5-branes probing A-type orbi-singularities in 11D supergravity, globally completed by flux-quantization in twisted equivariant differential (TED) Cohomotopy: Here the M-string ends of M2-branes on M5-branes engineer the FQH liquid's boundary. This geometric engineering on M-branes might naturally elucidate the curious combination of $W_\infty$-symmetry and of super-symmetry that is known to govern the collective excitations of FQH liquids at long wavelengths.
one_liner: Higher-gauge-theory framework derives fractional quantum Hall bulk-edge physics from a classifying fibration viewpoint.
why_it_matters: It gives a structured route to model protected chiral edge excitations that underpin robust topological quantum transport platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper reformulates bulk-edge correspondence in fractional quantum Hall systems as an effective relative higher gauge theory controlled by classifying fibrations. Using the complex Hopf fibration, it reconstructs chiral edge dynamics associated with Floreanini-Jackiw and Wess-Zumino-Witten descriptions without starting from a conventional Lagrangian. It further connects the effective theory to M2/M5-brane engineering and twisted equivariant differential cohomotopy, giving a mathematically unified picture of bulk topological order and boundary modes.
main_result: It identifies the complex Hopf fibration as the classifying structure for FQH bulk-boundary effects and derives the associated chiral edge theory within a higher-gauge framework.
application_angle: The framework can guide modeling of protected edge transport in topological quantum materials and engineered wave systems where boundary robustness is the practical resource.

title: Dynamically Reconfigurable Optical Skyrmions Enabled by a Silicon Microring Optical Phased Array for Robust Free-Space Communication
abstract: Optical skyrmions offer a robust vectorial information degree of freedom for free-space communication, but practical deployment requires a compact platform capable of active topological reconfiguration. Here, we propose a silicon microring-resonator optical phased array that integrates spin-selective emission and programmable phase control on a single chip. Optimized inner- and outer-grating microring emitters provide decoupled LCP and RCP radiation bases with polarization fractions of 90.27% and 91.40%, enabling active switching between Néel-type and Bloch-type skyrmions, while dynamically tuning the skyrmion number across Nsk =-1.914 to 1.918. Using these programmable topological states, a 4-symbol free-space communication link is constructed and compared with ideal LG-OAM encoding under Kolmogorov turbulence. The skyrmion-encoded link maintains a lower symbol error rate over a broader turbulence range, demonstrating that topological observables are more robust than scalar OAM modes. These results establish actively reconfigurable optical skyrmions as compact, programmable, and turbulence-tolerant information carriers for next-generation free-space optical communication.
one_liner: A silicon microring phased array generates reconfigurable optical skyrmions for turbulence-robust free-space links.
why_it_matters: It shows a compact silicon-photonics transmitter can use topological vector states that outperform scalar OAM encoding under turbulence.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a silicon microring-resonator optical phased array that combines spin-selective emission with programmable phase control on chip. Inner and outer grating microrings generate decoupled left- and right-circularly polarized bases with polarization fractions above 90%, enabling switching between Neel-type and Bloch-type skyrmions and tuning the skyrmion number from about -1.914 to 1.918. In a 4-symbol free-space link under Kolmogorov turbulence, skyrmion encoding yields lower symbol error rates than ideal LG-OAM encoding across a broader turbulence regime.
main_result: The device actively reconfigures optical skyrmion states on chip and uses them to realize a more turbulence-tolerant 4-symbol free-space communication link than OAM-based encoding.
application_angle: This points to compact, programmable transmitters for resilient free-space optical and potentially satellite communication in disturbed atmospheric channels.

title: Amplitude Modulation Noise Suppression of Dynamic Atom Gravimeters
abstract: Dynamic atom gravimeters enable absolute gravity measurements on moving platforms. However, their performance is severely degraded due to the complex dynamic environment. This paper finds that the amplitude modulation noise (AMN) is a key factor contributing to the degradation of gravity measurement performance. We find that the AMN is induced by the cold atomic cloud trajectory and velocity variation. We build a model to illustrate the principles and magnitude of AMN arising from various experiment processes. Then we propose a method to fit the normalized AMN respect to the kinematic parameters of the cold atomic cloud, and successfully suppress this noise from 0.11 to 0.038 using the fitting result. With this method, we improve the fringe phase resolution from 0.244 rad to 0.092 rad, and reduce the dynamic gravity measurement noise from 2.69 mGal to 1.68 mGal. This study finds and suppresses a key noise source for the dynamic atom gravimeters, which is important for further improving its precision. The proposed method can be also applied for precision enhancement for other dynamic atom interferometer-based sensors, such as the atom gradiometers and gyroscopes.
one_liner: A model-based AM noise correction cuts dynamic atom-gravimeter noise and improves phase resolution on moving platforms.
why_it_matters: Suppressing this previously underappreciated noise source directly improves deployable atom-interferometric gravity sensing outside the lab.
detailed_summary: The work identifies amplitude modulation noise as a major limiter in dynamic atom gravimeters and ties it to atomic-cloud trajectory and velocity variations. The authors build a quantitative model for AMN across the experimental sequence and fit normalized AMN as a function of cloud kinematics. Applying the correction reduces AMN from 0.11 to 0.038, improves fringe phase resolution from 0.244 rad to 0.092 rad, and lowers dynamic gravity noise from 2.69 mGal to 1.68 mGal.
main_result: A kinematics-based AM noise fitting and correction method substantially improves phase resolution and absolute gravity sensitivity in moving-platform atom gravimetry.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to navigation, geodesy, and field-deployed quantum inertial sensors, and should transfer to atom gradiometers and gyroscopes.

title: Quantum and classical processing with photonic quantum machine learning
abstract: Artificial intelligence and machine learning have been widely adopted both in the industry and in everyday life, but at the cost of high compute demands. Recent studies show that implementing machine learning in physical systems in the deep quantum regime could not only lead to faster information processing, but also to perform tasks that are out of reach for classical systems. Here, we report a quantum reservoir processing device capable of performing both quantum and classical machine learning tasks. The implementation is realized with a programmable silicon chip excited with single photons, a highly scalable and adaptable photonics technology. We successfully implement a variety of quantum tasks, including quantum state tomography and measurement of entanglement via negativity. Moreover, we implement a method of mitigation of experimental imperfections which results in a significant improvement in accuracy in comparison to the same system operating in the classical regime. Our results demonstrate a method to overcome a crucial bottleneck of quantum technologies by providing a practical way of probing quantum states.
one_liner: Single-photon silicon photonic reservoir hardware performs both quantum-state characterization and classical ML tasks.
why_it_matters: It turns programmable photonics into a practical processor for quantum-state probing, including tomography and entanglement estimation.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a programmable silicon photonic quantum reservoir processor driven by single photons. On the quantum side, it performs tasks including quantum state tomography and estimation of entanglement through negativity; on the classical side, it executes machine-learning tasks within the same hardware paradigm. The authors also introduce an error-mitigation method that significantly improves accuracy relative to operating the same system in a classical regime.
main_result: A silicon photonic reservoir device is shown to carry out both classical and quantum learning tasks, including tomography and entanglement measurement, with improved accuracy via error mitigation.
application_angle: This supports scalable photonic co-processors for characterization and control of quantum hardware, reducing the overhead of quantum-state verification in experiments.

title: Cavity-Enhanced Collective Quantum Processing with Polarization-Encoded Qubits
abstract: We introduce a cavity-enhanced optical architecture for collective quantum processing in which logical qubits are encoded in the polarization subspace of recirculating intracavity modes. The physical carrier and computational degree of freedom are explicitly separated: harmonic cavity bundles provide a stable resonant substrate, while programmable polarization transformations implement single-qubit operations. A polarization-selective nonlinear interaction in the entanglement region generates tunable controlled-phase gates, enabling a universal gate set. A parameter-scaling analysis shows that order-unity conditional phases are attainable in centimeter-scale cavities using experimentally accessible solid-state nonlinear media, without requiring extreme nonlinear coefficients, millisecond photon lifetimes, or sub-hertz laser stabilization. The results indicate that resonant recirculation provides a physically plausible platform for cavity based collective quantum architectures.
one_liner: An intracavity polarization-qubit architecture uses resonant recirculation and nonlinear optics for scalable collective quantum gates.
why_it_matters: It argues that useful controlled phases may be reachable in realistic cavity hardware without extreme materials or stabilization demands.
detailed_summary: The proposal encodes logical qubits in the polarization subspace of recirculating intracavity optical modes, separating the resonant carrier from the computational degree of freedom. Programmable polarization transformations implement single-qubit gates, while a polarization-selective nonlinear interaction region produces tunable controlled-phase operations. A scaling analysis indicates order-unity conditional phases are feasible in centimeter-scale cavities with accessible solid-state nonlinear media and without requiring extreme cavity lifetime or laser-stability specifications.
main_result: The architecture shows, by parameter scaling, that universal cavity-based quantum processing with order-unity conditional phases is physically plausible in experimentally realistic optical hardware.
application_angle: If realized, it offers a path toward compact photonic quantum processors that leverage cavity enhancement instead of demanding ultrahigh nonlinearity or ultralong photon storage.

title: Training continuously-coupled reconfigurable photonic chips with quantum machine learning
abstract: Integrated photonic technologies have recently shown significant advances, enabling the possibility to implement reconfigurable interferometers with increasing size. One of the main tasks to fully exploit the capabilities of reconfigurable integrated interferometers is the possibility to precisely program their operation to perform a desired target unitary. While recipes are known for circuit layouts based on a cascade of beam-splitter and phase-shifter operations, a methodology applicable for reconfigurable continuously-coupled waveguide arrays is currently missing. Here, we devise a machine learning based approach for this task, using a black box methodology that does not rely on precise a-priori modeling of the circuit internal architectures. We verify the effectiveness and the robustness of this approach via numerical simulations on different continuously-coupled waveguides layouts, either with planar or 3D structures. The proposed method makes use of a limited number of single- and two-photon measurements, making it suitable for optical quantum information processing. The obtained results open the perspective of employing this methodology as an effective tool to program the operation of integrated interferometers designed via different architectures.
one_liner: A black-box QML method trains continuously coupled photonic interferometers using limited one- and two-photon data.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical calibration bottleneck for integrated photonic chips whose internal coupling structure is hard to model analytically.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a machine-learning approach to program reconfigurable continuously coupled waveguide arrays toward a target unitary without requiring an accurate internal device model. The method is validated in numerical simulations across planar and three-dimensional photonic layouts and relies only on a limited set of single- and two-photon measurements. This makes it suitable for black-box calibration of integrated photonic processors beyond standard beam-splitter mesh architectures.
main_result: The authors show that target-unitary programming of continuously coupled photonic chips can be learned robustly from sparse quantum-optical measurements without detailed architectural knowledge.
application_angle: This could simplify commissioning and retuning of large photonic quantum circuits, improving manufacturability and field programmability of integrated processors.

title: Communication-Efficient Distributed Inverse Quantum Fourier Transform
abstract: The scalability of quantum computing is currently limited by physical, technological, and architectural constraints that hinder the integration of a large number of qubits within a single quantum processor. Distributed quantum computing (DQC) has therefore emerged as a viable alternative, aiming to interconnect multiple smaller quantum processing units (QPUs) to jointly operate on a global quantum state. While this paradigm enables scalable architectures, it introduces significant communication overhead due to the cost of non-local quantum operations across distant nodes. In this work we propose a distributed formulation of the iQFT over a quantum network composed of $P$ nodes, each hosting $Q$ qubits, enabling the execution on a logical register of size $n = P \cdot Q$. Furthermore, we introduce a communication-efficient variant based on a threshold-driven pruning strategy, referred to as a \emph{communication horizon}, which exploits the exponentially decreasing significance of controlled-phase rotations to safely omit remote gates with negligible impact. By reducing the number of inter-node quantum interactions, the proposed approach significantly lowers the quantum communication requirements of the distributed iQFT while preserving its functional correctness. Crucially, we show that this approach fundamentally alters the scaling of the algorithm: the entanglement resource consumption per node saturates to a constant value, reducing the global communication complexity from quadratic $\mathcal{O}(P^2)$ to linear $\mathcal{O}(P)$. As the iQFT constitutes a critical building block in many quantum algorithms, the techniques presented in this paper directly contribute to improving the practicality and scalability of distributed quantum computation.
one_liner: A distributed iQFT scheme prunes negligible remote phase gates to cut inter-node quantum communication from quadratic to linear scaling.
why_it_matters: It makes distributed quantum processors more practical by sharply reducing entanglement and communication overhead for a core algorithmic primitive.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates the inverse quantum Fourier transform across a network of P nodes with Q qubits each, acting on a logical register of size n=P·Q. It introduces a threshold-based communication horizon that omits remote controlled-phase gates whose contributions are exponentially small. This preserves functional correctness while changing the communication scaling from O(P^2) to O(P) and causing per-node entanglement resource use to saturate to a constant. Because iQFT appears in many larger algorithms, the result targets a major systems bottleneck in distributed quantum computing.
main_result: A communication-horizon variant of distributed iQFT achieves linear global communication complexity and constant per-node entanglement consumption.
application_angle: The method directly improves scalability for modular quantum computers executing phase-estimation- and Fourier-transform-based workloads over quantum networks.

title: Selective Placement of Hollow-Core Fibers for QKD and Classical Communication Coexistence
abstract: We investigate the benefits of partially upgrading optical networks with hollow-core fibers for QKD-classical communication coexistence. Results show that upgrading 40% of links in a metro topology can reduce the number of quantum modules by up to 49%.
one_liner: Strategic partial deployment of hollow-core fiber can substantially ease QKD and classical traffic coexistence in metro optical networks.
why_it_matters: It gives operators a concrete infrastructure-upgrade path that cuts QKD hardware demand without requiring a full network fiber replacement.
detailed_summary: This work studies selective replacement of conventional links with hollow-core fiber in optical networks carrying both QKD and classical channels. In a metro-topology analysis, upgrading only 40% of links yields up to a 49% reduction in the number of required quantum modules. The result suggests that the low-noise coexistence benefits of hollow-core fiber can be captured with targeted placement rather than uniform deployment. The emphasis is on network-level optimization rather than component-level physics alone.
platform_or_system: metro optical network with mixed standard and hollow-core fiber
main_result: Partial hollow-core-fiber placement can reduce required QKD modules by up to 49% while supporting coexistence with classical communications.
application_angle: The study is immediately relevant to cost-sensitive metro QKD rollout planning and phased telecom infrastructure upgrades.

title: Squeezing Enhancement Through Resonant Interference in Multi-ring Resonators
abstract: We develop a non-perturbative description of squeezed light generation in an arbitrary lossy structure consisting of multiple coupled microring resonators. This is applied to two ring photonic molecules where the interference of the fields in the coupled rings leads to a modification in the resonance spectrum near a shared resonance. Considering a dual-pump degenerate squeezing scheme under a five resonance approximation, we investigate two methods to suppress parasitic four-wave mixing contributions and compensate for group velocity dispersion within a primary resonator through hybridization effects with a second auxiliary resonator. In the former case, this comes from an effective splitting of the unwanted resonances supporting parasitic four-wave mixing interactions that add thermal noise to the desired degenerate squeezed state. For sufficiently strong coupling between the resonators, we demonstrate near complete suppression of such parasitic processes, resulting in near unit fidelities with the corresponding output state that would arise were the parasitic interactions neglected. In the latter case, the hybridization effectively shifts a pump resonance, realigning the desired dual-pump four-wave mixing process and leading to a significant enhancement of the signal generation and output squeezing.
one_liner: Coupled microring interference is used to suppress parasitic four-wave mixing and boost squeezed-light generation in integrated resonators.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical on-chip route to higher-fidelity and stronger squeezing by engineering resonances instead of relying only on material changes.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a non-perturbative theory for squeezed-light generation in arbitrary lossy networks of coupled microring resonators and apply it to two-ring photonic molecules. Under a dual-pump degenerate squeezing scheme, ring hybridization splits unwanted resonances that otherwise support parasitic four-wave-mixing channels adding thermal noise. For sufficiently strong coupling, these parasitic processes are nearly fully suppressed, giving near-unit fidelity relative to the ideal no-parasitic case. The same hybridization can also shift a pump resonance to compensate dispersion mismatch and significantly enhance signal generation and output squeezing.
main_result: Resonant interference in a two-ring photonic molecule can nearly eliminate parasitic four-wave mixing and enhance degenerate squeezing output.
application_angle: This is useful for integrated squeezed-light sources in quantum photonic sensing, communications, and fault-tolerant optical hardware.

title: No measurement induced phase transition in the entanglement dynamics of monitored non-interacting one-dimensional fermions in a disordered or quasiperiodic potential
abstract: We show that the entanglement entropy (EE) of one-dimensional (1d) non-interacting fermions with $U(1)$ symmetry in the presence of a quasi-periodic or disordered potential in which the occupation number is being monitored by homodyne or quantum jump protocols is always in an area-law phase so no measurement induced phase transition (MIPT) occurs. The reason for the previously claimed MIPT in these systems was a finite size effect related to the fact that the maximum lattice size $L \sim 500$ was of the order of the correlation length. By increasing the system size up to $L \leq 18000$, employing Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), and performing a careful finite size scaling analysis, we find that the critical monitoring strength is consistent with zero so no MIPT occurs. For the disordered case, these numerical results are fully supported by an analytical calculation based on mapping the problem onto a nonlinear sigma model (NLSM) with an additional mass-like term that confirms the absence of the MIPT for any monitoring or disorder strength. Another salient feature of the disordered case, in part related to a different symmetry in the NLSM, is that the correlation length in the weak disorder limit is longer than in the clean limit and increases with the disordered strength.
one_liner: Large-scale simulations show monitored disordered or quasiperiodic free-fermion chains remain area-law entangled, with no measurement-induced transition.
why_it_matters: It removes a misleading finite-size interpretation and clarifies the true phase structure relevant to monitored quantum simulators and hardware proposals.
detailed_summary: Using system sizes up to L<=18000 accelerated by GPUs, the paper revisits monitored one-dimensional non-interacting fermions with U(1) symmetry under homodyne or quantum-jump monitoring. The authors find the critical monitoring strength is consistent with zero, so the entanglement entropy stays in an area-law phase and no measurement-induced phase transition occurs. For the disordered case, an analytical nonlinear sigma model with an added mass-like term supports the numerics and likewise predicts no transition for any monitoring or disorder strength. The work also identifies an unusually long correlation length in the weak-disorder regime that helps explain earlier finite-size misclassification.
platform_or_system: monitored 1D disordered or quasiperiodic free-fermion chain
main_result: No measurement-induced phase transition is found; the monitored free-fermion systems remain area-law entangled across the studied parameter range.
application_angle: The result sharpens theoretical guidance for designing and interpreting monitored quantum simulation experiments, including photonic and atom-based platforms.

title: Passive optical superresolution at the quantum limit
abstract: For more than a century, the diffraction limit has defined the resolution achievable by passive optical imaging systems. Although some resolution improvement can be gained through classical data processing of the image, it is limited by the noise arising from quantum nature of light. Minimizing the effect of this noise requires quantum treatment of optical imaging. By reformulating imaging as a problem of quantum measurement and estimation, it becomes possible to identify optimal detection strategies that recover spatial information previously thought inaccessible. This review summarizes the theoretical framework that underpins this development, from the formulation of quantum Cramér-Rao bounds and Chernoff bounds to the construction of receivers that attain them, such as those based on spatial-mode demultiplexing. We show how these methods can beat conventional imaging in the classification, localization, and imaging of sub-Rayleigh incoherent sources. We then discuss extensions to multiparameter and partially coherent scenarios, and highlight the unifying connections between estimation and discrimination tasks. Finally, we survey recent experimental demonstrations that approach quantum-limited resolution and outline emerging applications in microscopy, astronomy, and optical sensing.
one_liner: This review maps how quantum-optimal measurements beat diffraction-limited imaging for sub-Rayleigh source localization and classification.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the measurement theory and experimental progress behind quantum-limited passive superresolution for real sensing applications.
detailed_summary: The article reviews passive optical superresolution from a quantum-estimation viewpoint, emphasizing quantum Cramer-Rao and Chernoff bounds and receivers that can attain them. Spatial-mode demultiplexing and related measurements are shown to recover information that direct imaging loses in the sub-Rayleigh regime. The review covers localization, classification, multiparameter estimation, partially coherent sources, and the link between estimation and discrimination tasks. It also surveys experiments approaching quantum-limited performance and discusses microscopy, astronomy, and optical sensing use cases.
platform_or_system: passive optical imaging with quantum-optimal mode-resolved receivers
main_result: Quantum measurement strategies such as spatial-mode demultiplexing can surpass conventional diffraction-limited direct imaging for incoherent source estimation.
application_angle: The framework is directly applicable to high-resolution microscopy, faint-object astronomy, and precision optical sensing without active illumination.

title: Algorithmic Advantage on a Gate-Based Photonic Quantum Neural Network
abstract: We report on a gate-based variational quantum classifier implemented with single photons and probabilistic gates, to emulate the standard quantum circuit model framework. We evaluate the expressive power of two deployable quantum neural networks (QNNs) by computing their effective dimension, a capacity measure grounded in a proven generalization-error bound, and compare them with classical artificial neural networks (ANNs) of equivalent trainable-parameter count. Supervised binary classification tasks are used to benchmark performance across photonic and superconducting QNNs, both of which exhibit superior converged (lower) cross-entropy loss and (higher) prediction accuracy relative to matched-parameter ANNs. For a nonlinearly separable task, our photonic QNN with a single pair of trainable parameters successfully converged (loss 0.04 and accuracy 100%), whereas the equivalent ANN failed to learn the decision boundary, saturating at random-guessing performance. We simulate photonic quantum circuits, training them on the XOR problem and a two-class Iris subset using gradient-free optimization, and assess their robustness to sampling errors under realistic noise processes including photon loss and phase-shifter imperfections. Circuits with comparatively high effective dimension were deployed remotely on a six-qubit photonic quantum processor, achieving classification accuracies of up to 100% in both online and offline learning settings. Notably, even the simplest QNN deployed, with just two trainable parameters, successfully solved tasks that classically require ANNs with at least quadruple the number of parameters, suggesting an emergent algorithmic advantage. Overall, these results demonstrate a clear proof-of-principle that gate-based QNNs can be realized and trained effectively on current photonic hardware, providing proof of algorithmic advantage on a gate-based photonic QNN.
one_liner: A gate-based photonic quantum neural network shows task-level algorithmic advantage with very few trainable parameters on real hardware.
why_it_matters: It is a concrete hardware demonstration that small photonic QNNs can outperform parameter-matched classical models on classification tasks.
detailed_summary: The paper implements a gate-based variational quantum classifier using single photons and probabilistic gates within the standard circuit model. Model capacity is quantified via effective dimension, and photonic and superconducting QNNs are benchmarked against classical ANNs with matched parameter counts on supervised binary classification tasks. A photonic QNN with only two trainable parameters solves a nonlinearly separable task where the equivalent ANN remains at chance, and remotely deployed circuits on a six-qubit photonic processor reach up to 100% accuracy in online and offline learning. The study also analyzes robustness to sampling noise, photon loss, and phase-shifter imperfections.
main_result: Real photonic QNN circuits achieved up to 100% classification accuracy and solved tasks that parameter-matched classical ANNs could not learn.
application_angle: This supports near-term photonic quantum machine learning for compact classifiers and motivates application-specific quantum inference on current hardware.

title: Emergence of synthetic twist defects in the surface code under local perturbation
abstract: Topologically-ordered quantum states with Abelian excitations can host defects that obey effective non-Abelian statistics, in principle allowing for quantum information processing via defect braiding. These extrinsic defects (or twists) are typically studied as static features of the lattice. However, an alternative proposal considers how an underlying topologically ordered quantum substrate can be locally perturbed to create and manipulate synthetic defects \cite{you_synthetic_2013}. Unfortunately, while largely referenced, elements of this proposal were never systematically studied. Understanding the energy spectrum is particularly important in finite size and finitely perturbed systems, which are crucial for experimental realizations. In this work we announce a significant step in this direction by explicitly constructing, simplifying, and numerically studying the spectral properties of synthetic defects in a model system. First, we introduce two alternative representations of this problem in both spin and Majorana languages. In the former we describe emergent virtual symmetries which constrain and simplify the problem and in the latter we show a direct connection to Kitaev's well-known Majorana chain. We utilize these simplifications to perform numerical calculations to indicate the location of the quantum phase transition driving the emergence of the synthetic defects. We conclude by discussing key steps for future work to more clearly and completely study this phenomena.
one_liner: This work maps locally perturbed surface-code twists to simplified spin and Majorana models and numerically locates the emergence transition.
why_it_matters: It sharpens how synthetic non-Abelian-like defects could be created and controlled in finite, experimentally relevant surface-code hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper studies how local perturbations in a surface-code-like topological phase can generate synthetic twist defects rather than treating twists as static lattice objects. It develops equivalent spin and Majorana descriptions, exposing virtual symmetries and a direct correspondence to a Kitaev Majorana chain that simplify the spectrum. Using these reductions, the authors numerically analyze the finite-size spectral structure and identify the quantum phase transition associated with defect emergence.
main_result: The authors construct tractable spin and Majorana formulations of synthetic surface-code twists and numerically indicate the transition where the defects emerge.
application_angle: Understanding finite-size spectra and control thresholds is directly relevant to implementing defect-based logical operations in realistic error-corrected quantum processors.

title: Qlustering for Data Clustering via Network-Based Quantum Transport
abstract: Analog quantum computation offers a route to machine learning using controllable physical dynamics as a computational resource. However, many existing approaches rely on task-specific protocols or observables that are difficult to access experimentally, limiting generality and implementation. Here we introduce Qlustering, an unsupervised clustering framework based on steady-state quantum transport in quantum networks governed by the GKSL master equation, developed through algorithm-hardware co-design. Data are encoded as input states, and cluster assignments are inferred from steady-state output currents, avoiding full state tomography in favor of accessible transport observables. The method realizes a hybrid classical-quantum workflow in which data preparation and training are performed classically, while clustering is carried out by transport dynamics. We benchmark the method on synthetic datasets, localization, and QM9 and Iris, finding competitive performance and stability over a broad range of dephasing strengths. These results show that unlabeled data structure can be extracted directly from steady-state transport observables, identifying terminal-current readout as a native, tomography-free mechanism for unsupervised learning in open quantum networks.
one_liner: Qlustering performs unsupervised clustering by reading terminal currents from open quantum networks at steady state.
why_it_matters: It proposes a tomography-free, hardware-native learning primitive based on transport observables that are accessible in analog quantum platforms.
detailed_summary: The method encodes data into input states of a quantum network evolving under a GKSL master equation, then infers cluster labels from steady-state output currents rather than reconstructed states. Training and data preparation remain classical, while the clustering step is delegated to dissipative transport dynamics in the quantum device. Benchmarks on synthetic sets, localization data, QM9, and Iris show competitive performance and robustness across a broad dephasing range.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that steady-state terminal-current readout can recover cluster structure competitively without full state tomography.
application_angle: Because it relies on current measurements instead of complex observables, the approach is better aligned with near-term analog network hardware for practical quantum data analysis.

title: Equivariant Reinforcement Learning for Clifford Quantum Circuit Synthesis
abstract: We consider the problem of synthesizing Clifford quantum circuits for devices with all-to-all qubit connectivity. We approach this task as a reinforcement learning problem in which an agent learns to discover a sequence of elementary Clifford gates that reduces a given symplectic matrix representation of a Clifford circuit to the identity. This formulation permits a simple learning curriculum based on random walks from the identity. We introduce a novel neural network architecture that is equivariant to qubit relabelings of the symplectic matrix representation, and which is size-agnostic, allowing a single learned policy to be applied across different qubit counts without circuit splicing or network reparameterization. On six-qubit Clifford circuits, the largest regime for which optimal references are available, our agent finds circuits within one two-qubit gate of optimality in milliseconds per instance, and finds optimal circuits in 99.2% of instances within seconds per instance. After continued training on ten-qubit instances, the agent scales to unseen Clifford tableaus with up to thirty qubits, including targets generated from circuits with over a thousand Clifford gates, where it achieves lower average two-qubit gate counts than Qiskit's Aaronson-Gottesman and greedy Clifford synthesizers.
one_liner: An equivariant RL agent synthesizes Clifford circuits near optimally and generalizes from small training sizes to much larger qubit counts.
why_it_matters: Fast, near-optimal Clifford synthesis reduces two-qubit overhead in compilation pipelines that matter for benchmarking, control, and fault-tolerant subroutines.
detailed_summary: The authors cast Clifford synthesis as an RL problem over symplectic matrix reductions to the identity and train via a random-walk curriculum from trivial instances. Their neural architecture is equivariant to qubit relabelings and size-agnostic, allowing one learned policy to transfer across system sizes without retraining the model shape. On six-qubit instances it reaches within one two-qubit gate of optimal in milliseconds and achieves 99.2% optimal synthesis within seconds, then scales after further training to unseen tableaus up to 30 qubits and outperforms Qiskit baselines on average two-qubit count.
main_result: The RL synthesizer achieves near-optimal and often optimal Clifford circuits quickly, while generalizing to much larger unseen qubit systems.
application_angle: Lower two-qubit gate counts and fast compile times can directly improve executable circuits on hardware where entangling gates dominate error and latency budgets.

title: Multi-Qubit Stabilizer Readout on a Dual-Species Rydberg Array
abstract: The ability to locally control and measure subsets of ancilla qubits in an efficient and crosstalk-free manner is a key ingredient in quantum error correction (QEC). Dual-species neutral atom arrays offer an ideal implementation of these capabilities, enabling independent state preparation, manipulation, and detection on each species. In this work, we realize such a dual-species Rydberg array of Na and Cs atoms trapped in co-localized 2D optical tweezer arrays, using Na as an ancilla to measure stabilizers of surrounding Cs data qubits. We identify the finite interspecies Rydberg-Rydberg interaction strength as a practical obstacle to high-fidelity multi-body entanglement and show that, by tuning the Rabi frequency and the detuning of the Rydberg driving field, the resulting geometric phase error can be compensated. This yields a protocol for simultaneous, non-destructive, in situ stabilizer readout of multiple data qubits via global pulses alone. Using this protocol, we demonstrate non-destructive measurement of Pauli-Z stabilizers on four-qubit Cs plaquettes via a single global Rydberg pulse sequence. Our results demonstrate dual-species tweezer arrays as a promising route towards scalable QEC and open the door to new quantum control protocols leveraging both interspecies and intraspecies interactions.
one_liner: A dual-species Na-Cs Rydberg array realizes global-pulse, nondestructive stabilizer readout of four-qubit plaquettes.
why_it_matters: Crosstalk-free ancilla control and in situ multi-qubit readout are core building blocks for scalable neutral-atom quantum error correction.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses co-localized 2D optical tweezer arrays holding Na ancillas and Cs data qubits, with Na designated for selective preparation and measurement. The authors identify finite interspecies Rydberg interaction strength as a source of geometric phase error and compensate it by tuning the drive Rabi frequency and detuning. With this correction, they implement simultaneous, nondestructive Pauli-Z stabilizer measurements on four-qubit Cs plaquettes using only global Rydberg pulse sequences.
main_result: The work demonstrates single-sequence, global-pulse, nondestructive readout of four-qubit stabilizers in a dual-species neutral-atom array.
application_angle: The protocol reduces control complexity and supports scalable syndrome extraction architectures for neutral-atom fault-tolerant processors.

title: A passive self-correcting quantum memory in three dimensions
abstract: We construct a 3D Pauli stabilizer Hamiltonian whose ground state space can encode a qubit for exponential time when coupled to a bath at non-zero temperature. Our construction recursively applies a sequence of transformations to a seed Hamiltonian that increases the memory lifetime of the encoded qubit while maintaining geometric locality in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
one_liner: The authors construct a 3D geometrically local stabilizer Hamiltonian with exponentially long passive quantum memory lifetime at nonzero temperature.
why_it_matters: A self-correcting memory would remove active error-correction overhead for stored quantum information, a major long-term goal in quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: This paper presents a three-dimensional Pauli stabilizer Hamiltonian whose ground-state manifold encodes a logical qubit with lifetime exponential in system parameters when coupled to a thermal bath. The construction is obtained recursively from a seed Hamiltonian through transformations that increase memory lifetime while preserving geometric locality in real 3D space. The result is a rare explicit route to passive finite-temperature protection in a physically local architecture.
main_result: The main result is a geometrically local 3D stabilizer model that stores a qubit for exponentially long times without active correction at nonzero temperature.
application_angle: If made physically realizable, such passive protection could drastically reduce the control and communication overhead needed for long-lived quantum memories and quantum repeater nodes.

title: Graph-State Circuit Blocks control Entanglement and Scrambling Velocities
abstract: Random circuit models often describe local dynamics using generic two-qubit gates, which have proven successful in capturing entanglement growth and operator spreading in many contexts. This approach naturally leads to the expectation that detailed gate structure plays only a limited role in coarse-grained entanglement and scrambling diagnostics. We show that the internal structure of multipartite circuit primitives can significantly influence these dynamical rates, even within a fixed random-circuit architecture. To investigate this, we study an exactly simulable family of Clifford quantum circuits built from fixed $n$-qubit graph-state preparation unitaries, which we treat as elementary building blocks. Specifically, we consider a one-dimensional chain of $N$ qubits initialized in a product state and evolved by layers in which nonoverlapping length-$n$ blocks are placed at uniformly random positions with sparsity $α$. We find that different choices of graph-state building blocks lead to strongly varying dynamical rates. Graph states that are inequivalent under local Clifford (LC) transformations generate sharply different entanglement velocities $v_E$ and butterfly velocities $v_B$, even though the circuits are drawn from the same ensemble with identical architecture and randomness parameters. We further show that this hierarchy is captured by two complementary block-level characteristics: the distribution of entanglement across internal bipartitions of the graph state, which correlates with $v_E$, and a graph-theoretic connectivity profile across bipartitions, which correlates with $v_B$. Neither descriptor alone fully determines the dynamics; rather, entanglement growth and operator spreading are controlled by distinct structural features of the local circuit blocks. Notably, AME states appear among the fastest scrambling building blocks within the ensembles studied here.
one_liner: Fixed multipartite graph-state blocks strongly tune entanglement and scrambling speeds in otherwise identical random Clifford circuits.
why_it_matters: It shows that local circuit primitive design, not just architecture, can materially affect dynamical performance in quantum processors and simulators.
detailed_summary: The study considers 1D random Clifford circuits built from nonoverlapping length-n graph-state preparation blocks inserted with sparsity alpha. Despite identical placement statistics and architecture, locally Clifford-inequivalent graph states produce sharply different entanglement velocities and butterfly velocities. The authors connect these rates to two block-level descriptors: internal bipartite entanglement structure, which tracks entanglement growth, and graph connectivity across bipartitions, which tracks operator spreading; AME states are among the fastest scramblers examined.
platform_or_system: Random Clifford circuits with graph-state blocks
main_result: Different multipartite graph-state primitives generate substantially different entanglement and scrambling rates even within the same random-circuit architecture.
application_angle: The results provide a design rule for choosing native circuit blocks in simulators and hardware where rapid entanglement generation or controlled scrambling is a practical objective.

title: Tolerating Device Failure in Distributed Quantum Computing
abstract: It is desirable that a distributed quantum computer can operate despite the replacement or failure of its constituent components, allowing the reliability of the distributed system to exceed that of its subcomponents. We first show that when quantum error correction is performed over a modular quantum network, quantum devices can be swapped out or replaced, during operation, with minimal impact on logical error rates. We also investigate the ability of the toric and hyperbolic Floquet quantum error correcting codes to protect logical information under low rates of modular node failure. In particular, we show that under the proposed distributed quantum error scheme, the selected codes are able to maintain good logical error suppression during the failure of entire nodes. For catastrophic node failure of probability p/100, we suggest that a distributed toric code would outperform one implemented on a monolithic device below a physical error rate of 0.05%.
one_liner: Shows distributed QEC can keep working through modular node swaps and even whole-node failures with limited logical-error penalty.
why_it_matters: Fault-tolerant distributed quantum computers need resilience to module outages; this gives concrete code-level evidence that networked architectures can degrade gracefully.
detailed_summary: The paper studies quantum error correction across modular quantum networks where constituent devices may be replaced during operation or fail entirely. It analyzes toric and hyperbolic Floquet codes under low rates of node failure and finds that logical error suppression can remain effective even when whole network nodes are lost. For catastrophic node failure probability p/100, the authors argue a distributed toric-code architecture can outperform a monolithic implementation when the physical error rate is below 0.05%.
platform_or_system: distributed modular quantum computer with topological codes
main_result: Distributed toric and Floquet-code schemes retain good logical protection under module replacement and low-rate full-node failures.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to scalable modular quantum computing, where serviceability, hot-swapping, and fault containment are essential for practical uptime.

title: Distributed estimation of many-body Hamiltonians via punctured surface code
abstract: We study how a punctured surface code can turn many local $Z$-type couplings into one protected logical signal for distributed quantum metrology, where the goal is to estimate a weighted average of the coupling strengths. We consider an ordinary planar patch with two $X$-cut holes and provide a distributed sensing protocol where all $Z$-type couplings correspond to the same nontrivial logical $\bar{Z}$ for the punctured surface code. When the couplings are disjoint, we show that the relevant global condition is equivalent to the existence of a closed dual loop, called a witness, that has an odd number of intersections with every chain. Together with a local clean opening condition, this witness criterion gives a concrete punctured-code construction in which all signal chains implement the same nontrivial logical $\bar Z$. For three-body interactions with overlapping supports, we also identify the class of interactions where our punctured surface code protocol applies. Overall, our results provide a novel, noise-robust distributed sensing protocol for many-body interactions, with corresponding topological design criteria.
one_liner: Uses a punctured surface code to convert many local many-body Z couplings into one protected logical sensing signal.
why_it_matters: It connects topological error correction with distributed metrology, offering a noise-robust route to aggregate weak spatially distributed signals.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a distributed sensing protocol on a planar surface-code patch with two X-cut holes, arranging many local Z-type couplings so they all implement the same logical Z operator. For disjoint couplings, they derive a witness-loop criterion on the dual lattice, plus a local opening condition, that guarantees the mapping to a common nontrivial logical operator. They also identify a class of overlapping three-body interactions compatible with the protocol, giving explicit topological design rules for protected many-body Hamiltonian estimation.
main_result: A punctured surface code can coherently and fault-tolerantly combine many spatially distributed local couplings into one logical observable for Hamiltonian estimation.
application_angle: The scheme could enable robust distributed field or interaction sensing on error-corrected quantum hardware without exposing the signal directly to local noise.

title: Plasmon exciton coupling enhances second order nonlinear response in borophene ZnO hybrid structures
abstract: Nonlinear optical processes in low dimensional materials are often weak or symmetry forbidden, limiting their use in nanoscale light sources and on chip frequency conversion. Here, we show that combining two weakly nonlinear systems, anisotropic borophene and excitonic zinc oxide, yields an enhanced and resonant nonlinear response. In borophene ZnO heterostructures, cathodoluminescence reveals a two orders of magnitude enhancement at 400 nm and 800 nm, due to an enhanced two photon absorption process. Under tunable near infrared excitation, a clear second harmonic signal emerges with quadratic power dependence and strong resonance near 800 nm. We attribute this to nonlinear plasmon exciton coupling, which reshapes the excitonic response and enables efficient hybrid pathways for frequency conversion. These results establish anisotropic plasmon exciton hybridization as a route to controlling nonlinear optical responses in low dimensional heterostructures.
one_liner: Borophene-ZnO hybrids show resonantly boosted SHG and two-photon absorption from plasmon-exciton coupling.
why_it_matters: The work offers a practical nanoscale route to strong nonlinear optics in low-dimensional materials where intrinsic second-order response is usually weak or forbidden.
detailed_summary: This study investigates anisotropic borophene coupled to excitonic ZnO in a hybrid heterostructure and measures the optical response using cathodoluminescence and tunable near-infrared excitation. The hybrid exhibits roughly two orders of magnitude enhancement at 400 nm and 800 nm, linked to enhanced two-photon absorption, and shows a clear second-harmonic signal with quadratic power dependence near 800 nm. The mechanism is attributed to nonlinear plasmon-exciton coupling that reshapes the excitonic response and opens efficient hybrid frequency-conversion pathways.
main_result: Hybridizing borophene plasmons with ZnO excitons strongly enhances second-order nonlinear response and enables resonant SHG around 800 nm.
application_angle: This could support compact on-chip frequency converters, nanoscale light sources, and tunable nonlinear photonic elements based on 2D hybrid materials.

title: Spatial overhead reduction for 2D hypergraph product codes
abstract: The hypergraph product creates a quantum stabilizer code from two input classical linear codes; a paradigmatic example being the surface code as a hypergraph product of two classical repetition codes. Many properties of the hypergraph product code can be inherited from those of the classical codes such as the code dimension, minimum distance and certain fault-tolerant gadgets. We investigate ways to reduce the number of physical qubits in hypergraph product codes while maintaining some of their useful properties for fault tolerance. We show that the code dimension, canonical logical basis, and minimum distances of the hypergraph product code are preserved through this reduction. We also provide distance-preserving syndrome measurement schedules as well as examples of reduced hypergraph product codes with parameter improvements such as $[\![610,64,6]\!] \rightarrow [\![441,64,6]\!]$ and $[\![1225,49,11]\!] \rightarrow [\![931,49,11]\!]$. In memory simulations with circuit-level depolarizing noise, we observe that the reduced codes can have similar subthreshold performance as their unreduced versions, but using fewer physical qubits. Finally, we show how overhead reduction can be compatible with homomorphic measurement gadgets, fold-transversal gates and automorphisms, which extends the savings to logical computation.
one_liner: Reduces qubit count in 2D hypergraph product codes while preserving code parameters and near-subthreshold memory performance.
why_it_matters: Overhead is a major blocker for fault-tolerant hardware, so qubit savings without losing distance or logical structure are immediately valuable.
detailed_summary: The paper develops reductions of 2D hypergraph product codes that lower physical-qubit count while preserving code dimension, canonical logical basis, and minimum distances. It also provides syndrome-measurement schedules that preserve distance and demonstrates concrete improvements such as [[610,64,6]] to [[441,64,6]] and [[1225,49,11]] to [[931,49,11]]. Circuit-level depolarizing-noise simulations show similar subthreshold memory behavior to unreduced codes, and the authors further show compatibility with homomorphic measurements, fold-transversal gates, and automorphism-based operations.
main_result: The authors achieve substantial spatial-overhead reduction in 2D HGP codes without sacrificing key code parameters or useful fault-tolerant operations.
application_angle: Lowering physical-qubit overhead directly improves the feasibility of implementing larger logical processors on near-term fault-tolerant hardware stacks.

title: Characterizing quantum correlations and quantum teleportation in $gg \to t\bar{t}$ and $q\bar{q} \to t\bar{t}$ processes under noisy channels
abstract: The measurement of top-quark spin correlations provides a key tool for probing its interactions with high precision. Owing to its extremely short lifetime ($τ\sim 10^{-25}$ s), the top quark preserves its spin polarization information, making the $t\bar{t}$ system an ideal framework for investigating quantum correlations in high-energy physics. In this work, we analyze quantum correlations in $t\bar{t}$ pairs produced in QCD using several quantum information-theoretic measures, including Bell nonlocality, quantum steering, concurrence, and geometric quantum discord. Their dependence on kinematic variables is examined in both the $gg \to t\bar{t}$ and $q\bar{q} \to t\bar{t}$ channels, with convergence toward the $gg \to t\bar{t}$ dominated regime in the ultra-relativistic limit ($β= 1$). We also investigate the effect of three effective decoherence channels (AD, PD, and PF). The AD and PD channels lead to a monotonic degradation of correlations as the decoherence parameter $p$ increases, while the PF channel exhibits a symmetric behavior around $p=1/2$. The impact of these channels on quantum teleportation is analyzed, showing that it remains above the classical threshold of $2/3$ even in the presence of noise. These results indicate that certain quantum resources can persist despite decoherence, opening new perspectives at the interface of quantum information and particle physics.
one_liner: Analyzes how noisy channels affect entanglement, steering, Bell signatures, and teleportation fidelity in top-quark pair states.
why_it_matters: It extends quantum-information diagnostics into high-energy processes and shows some usable quantum resources survive realistic decoherence models.
detailed_summary: The work evaluates Bell nonlocality, quantum steering, concurrence, and geometric discord for top-antitop pairs generated in gg -> ttbar and qqbar -> ttbar channels, tracking their dependence on kinematic variables. It then applies amplitude-damping, phase-damping, and phase-flip noise models, finding monotonic degradation for AD and PD and symmetry about p=1/2 for PF. Despite decoherence, the inferred teleportation fidelity remains above the classical 2/3 threshold, indicating persistent nonclassical correlations in these particle-physics states.
platform_or_system: top-quark pair spin states in QCD processes
main_result: Quantum-correlation measures in ttbar states remain nonclassical under several noisy channels, with teleportation fidelity staying above the classical limit.
application_angle: While not a deployable communication platform, it informs robust entanglement-resource analysis in extreme physical systems and cross-fertilizes methods from quantum communication.

title: TuniQ: Autotuning Compilation Passes for Quantum Workloads at Scale for Effectiveness and Efficiency
abstract: Quantum processors are being integrated into HPC ecosystems as co-processors, where compilation of quantum circuits into hardware-executable form determines both output fidelity and runtime. Current compilers use a fixed pass sequence and ignore the fact that optimal pass selection varies with circuit, hardware, and noise conditions. We present TuniQ, a reinforcement learning-based system that selects compilation passes at each pipeline stage, adapting to circuit, backend, and current noise profile. TuniQ introduces several novel design components like a dual-encoder for stage-aware representation, shaped rewards for cross-stage credit assignment, and dynamic action masking for valid compilation. Evaluated across diverse quantum workloads on multiple IBM Quantum Cloud processors, TuniQ improves fidelity and reduces compilation time over the state-of-the-art IBM Qiskit transpiler, generalizes across backends without retraining, and scales strongly to utility-scale circuits with growing advantage.
one_liner: RL-based compiler autotuning improves quantum-circuit fidelity and compile time across IBM backends and noise conditions.
why_it_matters: Compilation increasingly determines usable hardware performance, so adaptive pass selection is a high-leverage systems improvement for real devices.
detailed_summary: TuniQ is a reinforcement-learning system that chooses compilation passes stage by stage based on circuit structure, target backend, and current noise profile rather than using a fixed pipeline. Its design includes a dual encoder for stage-aware state representation, shaped rewards for cross-stage credit assignment, and dynamic action masking to enforce valid compiler actions. Across diverse workloads on IBM Quantum Cloud processors, it improves output fidelity and reduces compilation time versus the baseline Qiskit transpiler, while generalizing across backends without retraining.
platform_or_system: IBM Quantum processors with RL-tuned compilation
main_result: Adaptive RL-based pass selection outperforms fixed-sequence transpilation in both circuit quality and compilation efficiency on real IBM backends.
application_angle: This is immediately useful for production quantum workflows in HPC and cloud settings, where better compilation can raise effective hardware utility without changing the chip.

title: Classic and Quantum Task-Based Intelligent Runtime for QIRs Running on Multiple QPUs
abstract: High-performance computing systems are rapidly evolving into heterogeneous platforms that fuse quantum accelerators with traditional classical processing units (CPUs) and graphical processing units (GPUs). This convergence calls for runtimes capable of managing both classical and quantum workloads in a unified manner. We introduce an intelligent, task-based runtime that marries the Intelligent RuntIme System (IRIS) asynchronous scheduler with a quantum programming stack through the Quantum Intermediate Representation Execution Engine (QIR-EE). Our design allows programs written in the quantum intermediate representation (QIR) to be dispatched concurrently to a variety of back-ends, including multiple quantum simulators and nascent quantum processors, enabling genuine hybrid execution on a single node. To illustrate its practicality, we partition a 4-qubit and 20-qubit circuit into three sub-circuits using quantum circuit cutting via the QCut library. Each sub-circuit is simulated independently by the QIR-EE driver within IRIS, after which a classical post-processing step merges the simulation results to recover the outcome of the original full-circuit computation. This case study demonstrates how finer task granularity can enable the parallel execution and lower the simulation burden per quantum task while preserving overall accuracy, highlighting the feasibility of our hybrid approach.
one_liner: A task-based runtime executes QIR workloads concurrently across multiple QPUs and simulators for hybrid quantum-classical computing.
why_it_matters: It provides practical orchestration for heterogeneous quantum nodes, including circuit cutting workflows that trade monolithic runs for parallel sub-tasks.
detailed_summary: The work integrates the IRIS asynchronous task runtime with a Quantum Intermediate Representation Execution Engine so QIR programs can be scheduled alongside classical tasks on heterogeneous resources. It supports concurrent dispatch to multiple backends, including simulators and early quantum processors, within a unified runtime on a single node. As a case study, 4-qubit and 20-qubit circuits are partitioned into three subcircuits via QCut, simulated independently, and recombined classically to recover the original computation. The results show that finer-grained quantum tasks can reduce per-task simulation cost while preserving output accuracy.
platform_or_system: QIR runtime on heterogeneous multi-QPU/HPC nodes
main_result: The system demonstrates concurrent multi-backend QIR execution and successful circuit-cutting reconstruction for partitioned 4-qubit and 20-qubit workloads.
