application_angle: This is relevant for near-term hybrid quantum computing because it improves how real workflows are mapped, split, and scheduled across limited quantum resources.

title: Correlations Between Quantum Battery Capacity and Quantum Resources for Two-qubit System
abstract: We investigate the relationship between quantum battery capacity and quantum resources in a two-qubit system consisting of mutually coupled battery and charger subsystems. We find that the battery capacity decreases monotonically with the quantum entanglement, steering, Bell nonlocality and coherence, and peaks when these four quantum resources vanish. Moreover, we reveal the capacity gap between the total system capacity and the sum of the battery and charger spin capacities, which is the residual battery capacity, and establish its positive correlation with entanglement. Furthermore, unlike the first four resources, although the battery capacity decreases monotonically with quantum imaginarity, its disappearance under system detuning does not guarantee a peak capacity, and this effect becomes more pronounced as the detuning increases. In contrast to the first five resources, the quantum state texture shows a positive correlation with battery capacity, but a negative correlation with entanglement, steering, Bell nonlocality, coherence, imaginarity, and residual battery capacity. These monotonic relationships are independent of the choice of system parameters. Our findings reveal the relationship between quantum battery capacity and quantum resources during the dynamic evolution of a quantum battery system, and advances the theory of quantum batteries and the development of quantum energy storage systems.
one_liner: A two-qubit quantum battery study maps how charging capacity tracks entanglement, coherence, imaginarity, and state texture.
why_it_matters: It identifies which quantum resources help or hinder stored energy, giving design guidance for controllable small-scale quantum energy devices.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a coupled two-qubit charger-battery model and compares battery capacity against several quantum-resource measures during dynamical evolution. Capacity is found to decrease monotonically with entanglement, steering, Bell nonlocality, coherence, and also imaginarity, while a residual capacity term correlates positively with entanglement. In contrast, quantum state texture correlates positively with capacity and negatively with the other resource indicators. The reported monotonic trends are robust against changes in system parameters, with detuning highlighting a distinct behavior for imaginarity.
main_result: The main result is a parameter-robust set of correlations showing that maximal battery capacity occurs when several standard quantum resources vanish, while state texture behaves oppositely.
application_angle: These correlations can inform how small quantum energy-storage elements are biased and controlled to maximize usable capacity rather than abstract resource measures.

title: Loss-induced quantum nonreciprocity and entanglement in superconducting qubits
abstract: Losses are ubiquitous in physics and are usually regarded as harmful in quantum information processing. Here, we propose a loss-induced scheme to achieve nonreciprocity and nonreciprocal entanglement in a superconducting platform, where two remote superconducting transmon qubits are connected via two lossy auxiliary cavities. The nonreciprocity in our scheme originates from interference between multiple lossy coupling paths. The coherent phases associated with the qubit-resonator couplings reverse sign under propagation reversal, while the loss-induced phases remain direction independent. Their combined effect leads to different interference conditions in the opposite directions, resulting in unequal effective couplings. We show that this loss-induced scheme can generate nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, indicating that loss can be utilized as a resource. Moreover, the tunability of nonreciprocity and nonreciprocal entanglement in our scheme can be manipulated by the relative phase induced by loss, allowing to tailor both reciprocal and nonreciprocal behaviors. Our results establish a direct link between engineered loss and nonreciprocal entanglement in quantum information processing and offer potential applications in scalable quantum networks.
one_liner: Engineered loss in a superconducting circuit creates directional effective coupling and nonreciprocal entanglement between remote transmons.
why_it_matters: It turns dissipation from a liability into a design resource for directional quantum links and asymmetric entanglement generation.
detailed_summary: Two remote transmon qubits are coupled through two lossy auxiliary cavities, producing multiple dissipative coupling paths whose interference depends on propagation direction. The directional asymmetry arises because coherent coupling phases reverse under path reversal while loss-induced phases do not, yielding unequal effective couplings. Within this framework, the authors show both tunable nonreciprocal transport and nonreciprocal entanglement generation. The relative loss-induced phase provides a handle to move between reciprocal and nonreciprocal regimes.
platform_or_system: remote transmons linked by lossy auxiliary cavities
main_result: The scheme demonstrates that engineered cavity loss can generate tunable nonreciprocity and direction-dependent entanglement in a superconducting-qubit platform.
application_angle: Such directional couplers could be useful for modular superconducting networks, protected routing, and asymmetric quantum state transfer between nodes.

title: Breaking the scalability barrier via a vertical tunable coupler in 3D integrated transmon system
abstract: Scaling superconducting quantum processors beyond the constraints of monolithic planar architectures is essential for fault-tolerant quantum computation. Here we demonstrate a three-dimensional (3D) integrated superconducting quantum processor in which two qubit chips are vertically stacked on opposing sides of a carrier chip and galvanically connected via multilayer flip-chip bonding. Intrachip qubit coupling is mediated by planar tunable couplers, whereas interchip coupling is enabled by vertical tunable couplers embedded in the carrier chip. Randomized benchmarking reveals simultaneous single-qubit gate fidelities of 99.87 % with negligible crosstalk, and controlled-Z gates achieve an average fidelity of 97.5 % for both intrachip and interchip operations. We further demonstrate high-fidelity Bell-state preparation and coherent generation of a four-qubit $W$ state, confirming the architecture's capability for interchip entanglement distribution. These results establish vertical coupling as a promising pathway toward scalable quantum processors compatible with advanced quantum error-correcting codes.
one_liner: A vertically coupled 3D transmon stack achieves high-fidelity interchip CZ gates and multipartite entanglement across chips.
why_it_matters: It addresses superconducting scaling limits by showing that vertically integrated chips can preserve gate quality while enabling modular growth.
detailed_summary: The processor stacks two qubit chips on opposite sides of a carrier chip and uses multilayer flip-chip bonding for galvanic connection. Planar tunable couplers provide intrachip interactions, while vertical tunable couplers embedded in the carrier mediate interchip coupling. The reported performance includes simultaneous single-qubit randomized-benchmarking fidelities of 99.87% with negligible crosstalk and average CZ fidelity of 97.5% for both intra- and interchip gates. Bell-state generation and a coherent four-qubit W state verify entanglement distribution across the stacked architecture.
main_result: The work demonstrates near-parity between intrachip and interchip control, including 97.5% CZ gates and cross-chip multipartite entanglement in a 3D transmon system.
application_angle: Vertical tunable coupling offers a credible route to modular superconducting processors that can scale beyond planar layouts and support error-corrected architectures.

title: QuBridge: Layer-wise Fidelity Decomposition in Quantum Computation Pipeline
abstract: Running a quantum circuit on current hardware involves a sequence of engineering decisions, each with tunable parameters and distinct error characteristics. Existing tools optimize each decision in isolation, leaving practitioners unable to determine how much each decision contributes to final output quality. We present QuBridge, a pipeline analysis tool that decomposes quantum computation into three decision layers and measures each layer's fidelity contribution through progressive ablation and isolation experiments. Applied to quantum teleportation under IBM-calibrated noise models, the framework surfaces three phenomena that end-to-end measurement obscures. Qubit selection narrows the worst-case fidelity band from 11.8% to under 2% with downstream layers held fixed, without changing the peak. Per-gate pulse-shape assignment adds a +0.9% residual gain whose attributed magnitude depends on upstream layout. Error-detection encoding is not uniformly advantageous, and its conditional benefit emerges for input states whose dominant error channel is detectable by the chosen code. QuBridge operates on cached calibration data without requiring live hardware access.
one_liner: QuBridge decomposes output fidelity into contributions from qubit selection, pulse choices, and encoding across a quantum execution pipeline.
why_it_matters: It gives practitioners a way to quantify which engineering choices actually dominate performance before using live hardware time.
detailed_summary: QuBridge analyzes three decision layers in the quantum computation pipeline using progressive ablation and isolation experiments rather than only end-to-end fidelity. On quantum teleportation with IBM-calibrated noise models, it shows qubit selection can compress worst-case fidelity spread from 11.8% to below 2% while leaving the peak unchanged. It also attributes an additional 0.9% residual gain to per-gate pulse-shape assignment, with the measured benefit depending on upstream layout. Error-detection encoding is shown to help only conditionally, especially when the dominant error channel is detectable by the chosen code.
platform_or_system: pipeline analysis under IBM-calibrated noise models
main_result: The framework isolates layer-specific fidelity effects and reveals that layout, pulse assignment, and encoding contribute in distinct and state-dependent ways.
application_angle: This is useful for hardware users and networked protocol designers who need to choose compilation and control settings systematically without repeated live-device experiments.

title: Telecom quantum memory over one microsecond in nanophotonic lithium niobate
abstract: Nanophotonic quantum memory is a vital component for scalable quantum information processing for quantum computing, networking, and sensing applications. We store single-photon-level telecom-band optical pulses for more than a microsecond using an atomic frequency comb in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate, well beyond what is practically feasible via propagation in even the best nanophotonic devices due to propagation losses. We verify the quantum nature of this storage by demonstrating the phase coherence and sub-single-photon noise upon retrieval. We also show the flexibility of our platform by storing up to 20 temporal modes and demonstrating an acceptance bandwidth up to 2.2 GHz. These results establish erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate as a practical platform for on-chip quantum memory at telecom wavelengths, a key missing element for photonic quantum computing and quantum networking.
one_liner: An on-chip telecom quantum memory in erbium-doped lithium niobate stores single-photon-level pulses for over 1 microsecond.
why_it_matters: It fills a major gap for integrated photonic quantum systems by combining telecom compatibility, long storage, multimode capacity, and GHz bandwidth.
detailed_summary: The device uses an atomic frequency comb protocol in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate to store telecom-band optical pulses at the single-photon level. Storage exceeds one microsecond, well beyond what propagation delay lines in nanophotonic devices can practically provide given optical loss. The authors verify quantum operation through preserved phase coherence and sub-single-photon added noise on retrieval. The platform also supports up to 20 temporal modes and acceptance bandwidth up to 2.2 GHz, indicating both multiplexing and high-throughput capability.
platform_or_system: erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate nanophotonic memory
main_result: The main result is >1 microsecond telecom-band single-photon-level storage with coherent, low-noise retrieval plus 20-mode and 2.2 GHz operation on chip.
application_angle: This directly supports photonic quantum repeaters, network nodes, and integrated optical quantum processors that need native telecom-wavelength buffering and synchronization.

title: General Criteria for Certifying Genuine High-Dimensional Quantum Teleportation
abstract: Developing reliable methods for certifying the dimension of a given quantum system or process is essential to ensure the validity of claimed realization of high-dimensional (HD) quantum advantages. The existing criteria for certifying genuine HD quantum teleportation (HDQT) mainly focus on demonstrating the successful transmission of genuine HD quantum states. However, a complete certification of HDQT must also identify the entanglement dimension of resource, which is critical for verifying whether the transmission capacity and noise resilience meet the necessary thresholds. Here we propose two universal criteria (based on fidelity and robustness, respectively) for certifying genuine HDQT behaviors that can close this gap by fully identifying the dimension of the entanglement. Both criteria require only the input and output teleportation data and remain feasible under partial Bell-state measurements. Furthermore, the robustness-based criterion has stronger noise resistance and it requires no prior assumptions about local operations, making it robust even in black-box scenario. Our results establish a universal and reliable theoretical framework for validating the core quantum advantage in HDQT, pivotal for ensuring the reliable links in HD quantum networks.
one_liner: Introduces universal fidelity- and robustness-based tests that certify genuine high-dimensional quantum teleportation including entanglement dimension.
why_it_matters: High-dimensional teleportation claims need dimension-certified entanglement, and these criteria provide a practical way to verify it from input-output data alone.
detailed_summary: The paper develops two general certification criteria for genuine high-dimensional quantum teleportation, one based on teleportation fidelity and one on robustness. Unlike earlier approaches that mainly certify transmission of high-dimensional states, these criteria also identify the entanglement dimension of the resource state. Both use only observed input-output teleportation statistics and remain applicable under partial Bell-state measurements. The robustness criterion is especially notable because it tolerates more noise and does not assume trusted local operations, making it suitable for black-box network settings.
main_result: The authors derive universal data-driven criteria that certify genuine high-dimensional teleportation while explicitly bounding the underlying entanglement dimension.
application_angle: This strengthens verification for high-capacity quantum network links where dimensionality directly affects channel capacity, resilience, and trustworthiness.

title: Laser-assisted tunneling in a static tungsten diselenide WSe$_2$ barrier
abstract: We study the tunneling effect of Dirac fermions in a monolayer WSe$_2$ subjected to a static electrostatic barrier and irradiated by a linearly polarized laser field. Within the Floquet formalism, the time-periodic driving is incorporated to derive analytical wave functions across the three regions of the system. By enforcing continuity conditions at the interfaces, we obtain the transmission and reflection coefficients, which are then used to evaluate the conductance via the Büttiker approach. Our results reveal that the laser field induces a rich Floquet sideband structure, whose number and strength increase with the driving parameter $α$. This leads to a significant suppression of transmission and provides an efficient mechanism to overcome Klein tunneling. Moreover, increasing the width of the irradiated region enhances the interaction between fermions and the external field, resulting in energy renormalization and the formation of Stark-like confined states. The interaction between several Floquet channels creates strong interference effects, which reduce the transmitted current even further. The results demonstrate that light-matter interaction allows for the dynamic control of quantum transport in WSe$_2$ materials. This technology allows for the development of new optoelectronic devices, including tunable quantum filters and light-controlled nanoscale transistors.
one_liner: Floquet driving of a WSe2 barrier strongly suppresses Dirac-fermion transmission and enables optically tunable quantum transport.
why_it_matters: It shows a route to light-controlled transport in 2D materials, relevant for tunable quantum filters and optoelectronic switching devices.
detailed_summary: Using a Floquet treatment, the work analyzes Dirac-fermion tunneling through a static electrostatic barrier in monolayer WSe2 under linearly polarized laser irradiation. The periodic drive generates multiple Floquet sidebands whose weights grow with the driving parameter, leading to strong transmission suppression and mitigation of Klein tunneling. Increasing the irradiated width further enhances field-matter coupling, producing energy renormalization and Stark-like confined states. Interference among Floquet channels reduces transmitted current, giving a controllable handle on conductance.
main_result: The study shows that optical driving can reshape the sideband spectrum and strongly reduce transmission, enabling dynamic control of conductance through a WSe2 barrier.
application_angle: Light-tunable transport control in WSe2 is relevant for nanoscale optoelectronic modulators, quantum filtering, and field-programmable sensing elements.

title: Security of decoy-state quantum key distribution with correlated bit-and-basis encoders
abstract: Practical quantum key distribution (QKD) modulators inevitably introduce correlations, causing the state emitted in a given round to depend on the setting choices made in previous rounds. These correlations break the round-by-round independence structure on which many widely used security proof techniques rely, leaving a significant gap between available theoretical guarantees and the reality of practical implementations. In this work, we develop a finite-key security proof for decoy-state BB84 against general coherent attacks that rigorously incorporates correlations introduced by Alice's bit-and-basis encoder, while requiring only partial characterization of such correlations.
one_liner: Presents a finite-key decoy-state BB84 security proof that explicitly handles correlated bit-and-basis encoding in practical modulators.
why_it_matters: Real QKD hardware has memory effects, and this closes a key theory-practice gap by proving security under more realistic source correlations.
detailed_summary: The paper addresses a major practical issue in QKD: emitted states may depend on previous encoder settings because of modulator-induced correlations. These temporal correlations invalidate standard round-independent security proof techniques used in many decoy-state BB84 analyses. The authors provide a finite-key security proof against general coherent attacks that rigorously incorporates correlations from Alice's bit-and-basis encoder while requiring only partial source characterization. This makes the security framework substantially closer to deployed hardware behavior.
main_result: The main result is a finite-key security proof for decoy-state BB84 that remains valid under practical encoder correlations and general coherent attacks.
application_angle: This improves confidence in fielded QKD systems by allowing rigorous key extraction analysis for imperfect modulators with memory effects.

title: Realization of Backward Retrieval in a Stark-modulated Spin-wave Quantum Memory
abstract: We report the first experimental realization of backward retrieval in a spin-wave quantum memory based on a Stark-echo-modulated protocol in Eu3+:Y2SiO5. By using Stark control, we preserve the full optical depth of the ensemble while suppressing coherent noise, enabling conditional storage fidelities above 97%. Our analysis shows that the present backward-retrieval efficiency is mainly limited by technical imperfections rather than by fundamental constraints. With realistic engineering improvements, backward retrieval in this protocol could move beyond the reabsorption-limited forward-emission regime. The protocol is also compatible with cavity-enhanced operation, offering an additional route toward higher efficiencies. These findings establish Stark-echo modulation as a practical and scalable route to high-efficiency, long-lived solid-state quantum memories.
one_liner: Demonstrates the first backward retrieval in a Stark-echo spin-wave quantum memory using Eu3+:Y2SiO5.
why_it_matters: Backward retrieval is a key step toward higher-efficiency solid-state quantum memories for repeaters and network nodes.
detailed_summary: This experiment realizes backward retrieval in a Stark-echo-modulated spin-wave quantum memory hosted in Eu3+:Y2SiO5. Stark control suppresses coherent noise without sacrificing the ensemble's full optical depth, yielding conditional storage fidelities above 97%. The current backward-retrieval efficiency is identified as technically rather than fundamentally limited, indicating clear room for improvement. The protocol is also compatible with cavity enhancement, which could push performance beyond forward-emission reabsorption limits.
main_result: The authors experimentally achieve backward retrieval with high conditional fidelity in a solid-state Stark-echo quantum memory.
application_angle: Efficient, long-lived solid-state memories with backward retrieval are directly relevant to quantum repeater architectures and multiplexed network nodes.

title: Towards digital phantoms: emulating scattering with a spatial light modulator
abstract: The distortion of light's degrees of freedom when passing through complex random media is of great interest across a diversity of fields, e.g., scattering in biological studies. Emulating such media in a controlled laboratory setting conventionally relies on real-world physical samples (e.g., white paint), inhomogeneous mixtures with embedded scatterers, or biological tissue-mimicking phantoms. Such methods, while effective in certain contexts, are not without complexity and limitations: the exact medium properties are challenging to control and often require laborious preparation, external characterisation techniques, are not easily reproducible between studies and cannot be matched precisely by numerical simulations. Here, we propose a simple all-digital implementation of random scattering which can be readily implemented on any setup capable of producing digital holograms. Our approach employs binary random phase masks encoded onto a spatial light modulator which perturbs the input beam's phase and amplitude. We highlight two methods to precisely tune distortion strengths which show excellent agreement between simulated and measured results. We demonstrate distortion strengths comparable to real-world scattering samples and illustrate two example applications to emulate scattering of scalar and vectorial structured light. Finally we showcase the versatility of this toolkit for emulating various amplitude and phase profiles and suggest several easy to implement alternative modalities accessible with this method. This digital phantom circumvents many of the practical challenges of physical samples, making it ideally suited for applications at the intersection of structured light, biological imaging and optical communications.
one_liner: Uses an SLM with binary random phase masks to emulate controllable optical scattering as a fully digital phantom.
why_it_matters: It replaces hard-to-control physical scattering samples with a reproducible, simulation-matched digital tool for imaging and communications experiments.
detailed_summary: The authors propose an all-digital scattering emulator based on binary random phase masks displayed on a spatial light modulator. The method perturbs both phase and amplitude of the incident beam and includes two calibration approaches for precisely tuning the distortion strength, with strong agreement between simulation and experiment. The demonstrated distortions reach levels comparable to real scattering media and are applied to both scalar and vector structured light. The framework also supports flexible emulation of different amplitude and phase profiles without sample preparation overhead.
main_result: The work demonstrates a reproducible SLM-based digital phantom that accurately emulates tunable optical scattering with experimentally validated control.
application_angle: This is useful for rapid prototyping and benchmarking in structured-light imaging, tissue-like optical testing, and scattering-aware communication links.

title: Versatile probe state preparation via generalized measurements for quantum sensing and thermometry
abstract: We investigate a probe state preparation protocol based on two non-selective generalized quantum measurements to enhance parameter estimation in single-qubit systems. By fine-tuning the measurement strengths, we demonstrate the ability to design a broad class of probe states, initially prepared in a thermal state, which can be optimized for specific estimation tasks. We apply this framework to characterize the decay rate and the temperature of a generalized amplitude damping channel. Our results show that the preparation protocol significantly modulates the quantum Fisher information for both parameters. Furthermore, we derive a general analytical relationship between the quantum Fisher information, thermodynamic susceptibilities, and Hamiltonian variance, valid even in the transient regime. This connection highlights the role of energy fluctuations and kinetic response in determining metrological precision. Finally, we briefly discuss a quantum circuit for experimental implementation using nuclear magnetic resonance techniques.
one_liner: Shows that two generalized non-selective measurements can prepare sensing-optimized single-qubit probe states from thermal inputs.
why_it_matters: Measurement-based probe engineering offers a practical route to improve thermometry and dissipative parameter estimation without requiring pure-state initialization.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a probe-preparation protocol where two non-selective generalized measurements reshape an initially thermal single-qubit state for metrology. By tuning the measurement strengths, the protocol generates a broad family of probe states and modulates the quantum Fisher information for estimating both the decay rate and temperature of a generalized amplitude damping channel. The authors also derive an analytical relation linking quantum Fisher information to thermodynamic susceptibilities and Hamiltonian variance, including transient nonequilibrium regimes. An NMR-oriented circuit sketch is provided to support experimental implementation.
platform_or_system: single-qubit thermal probes under generalized measurements
main_result: The main result is that sequential generalized measurements can engineer thermally initialized probe states with improved sensitivity for decay-rate and temperature estimation.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant for practical quantum thermometry and noise sensing where thermal starting states and measurement-based control are easier than full coherent state preparation.

title: Strong light enhancement by combining the photonic nanojet and plasmons from the nano-engineered microsphere
abstract: Today's cutting-edge optical spectroscopic exploratory tools, such as Raman or infrared spectroscopy, rely on methods of signal enhancement as a route for their development. These methods are indispensable for substance identification and characterization in almost any scientific, regulatory, or industrial laboratory, therefore new and better methods of enhancement are always sought after. In this paper, the design of a new optical device for enhancement is presented, called nano-engineered microsphere (NMS). This device innovatively combines plasmons, a present flagship enhancement method, with a photonic nanojet, a new and emerging enhancement tool, to provide unprecedented properties in terms of affordability, stability, and performance. By using numerical simulations, a detailed design of the device is presented, and the optimization of device parameters for the strongest enhancement is investigated. The simulations show different influences of the parameters on the enhancement, from low to critical. The most influential parameter was found to be the radius of the nanoelement tip, which, at low values, showed a tremendous increase in the enhancement. The optimized device shows exceptionally promising abilities regarding the enhancement, while the estimated cost of production and use is low. Such properties paired with low price and ease of usage could enable the NMS to become one of the leading methods of enhancement in Raman and infrared spectroscopy with the spatial resolution towards nanometers.
one_liner: A nano-engineered microsphere combines photonic nanojets and plasmonic hotspots for strong, low-cost optical field enhancement.
why_it_matters: It points to a practical route for boosting Raman and IR spectroscopy sensitivity with inexpensive, stable structures that could approach nanoscale spatial resolution.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a nano-engineered microsphere that integrates a photonic nanojet produced by the microsphere with plasmonic enhancement from a nanoscale engineered tip. Using numerical simulations, the authors optimize geometric parameters and identify the nanoelement tip radius as the dominant lever for enhancement, with small radii yielding dramatic gains. The resulting structure is positioned as a low-cost, robust enhancement platform for spectroscopy rather than a fragile single-use substrate.
main_result: Numerical optimization predicts exceptionally strong optical enhancement from a microsphere device that synergistically combines photonic nanojets and plasmonic concentration.
application_angle: The design is directly relevant to higher-sensitivity Raman and infrared spectroscopy for chemical identification in laboratory and industrial settings.

title: Enabling Deterministic Passive Quantum State Transfer with Giant Atoms
abstract: Achieving quantum state transfer in passive ways can become a powerful asset for scalable quantum networks. Here, we demonstrate how giant atoms coupled to 1D waveguides provide a platform for such a passive, deterministic transfer. Engineering the position and strength of coupling points, we show that the nonlocal interaction can be utilized for the emission of time-reversal-symmetric single-photon wavepackets by spontaneous decay. We first derive general analytical conditions under which arbitrary qubit decays can be mapped to wavevector-dependent couplings that guarantee perfect state transfer in the continuum limit of infinitely many coupling points. Then, for experimentally relevant configurations with a finite number of coupling points, we demonstrate that high transfer fidelities can still be achieved by optimization, reaching 87% with only two coupling points and exceeding 99% with ten or more. We further analyze the robustness of the protocol against disorder in leg positioning and extend the formalism to environments with nonlinear dispersion, showing that dispersion-induced distortions can be fully compensated by judiciously chosen setups. Our results establish giant atoms as a powerful platform for realizing high-fidelity quantum state transfer in a setting without time-dependent control, opening new avenues for scalable quantum networks and engineered light-matter interfaces.
one_liner: Giant atoms in 1D waveguides can passively emit tailored photons that enable near-deterministic quantum state transfer.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-efficient path to scalable quantum links by replacing active pulse shaping and control with engineered light-matter coupling geometry.
detailed_summary: The authors derive analytical conditions under which a decaying qubit coupled as a giant atom to a 1D waveguide emits time-reversal-symmetric single-photon wavepackets suitable for perfect passive transfer. In the ideal continuum limit, arbitrary qubit decays can be mapped to wavevector-dependent couplings that guarantee deterministic transfer. For finite coupling-point implementations, optimization yields 87% fidelity with two points and above 99% with ten or more, while also compensating nonlinear-dispersion distortions and tolerating placement disorder.
main_result: The work shows that engineered giant-atom couplings enable passive quantum state transfer with fidelity exceeding 99% in realistic finite-point configurations.
application_angle: This could simplify node-to-node quantum interconnects for superconducting or waveguide-based quantum networks by eliminating time-dependent control overhead.

title: Observation of Magnetically-Induced atomic transitions of the Cs 6S$_{1/2} \rightarrow 7$P$_{3/2}$ line at 456 nm
abstract: It has recently been demonstrated that magnetically induced (MI) transitions, a class of transitions forbidden at zero magnetic field, of the Cs 6$^2$S$_{1/2} \rightarrow 6^2$P$_{3/2}$ (D$_2$) line, exhibit promising features for high-resolution physics applications in the near-infrared range. In this work, we study a group of seven MI transitions ($F_g = 3 \rightarrow F_e = 5$) of the Cs $6^2$S$_{1/2} \rightarrow 7^2$P$_{3/2}$ line at $λ= 456$ nm. The experimental measurements are in very good agreement with theoretical predictions based on the diagonalization of the Zeeman Hamiltonian. In magnetic fields ranging from $0.2-3$ kG, these transitions reach a maximum intensity above that of conventional transitions. Another noteworthy property is their large frequency shift, reaching approximately $17~\mathrm{GHz}$ with respect to the unperturbed hyperfine transitions in magnetic fields of about $3~\mathrm{kG}$. These interesting properties may prove useful for the realization of optical frequency references or magnetometers with sub-micron spatial resolution in the blue region of the spectrum.
one_liner: Cesium 456 nm magnetically induced transitions become strong and highly field-shifted, enabling blue-region precision sensing.
why_it_matters: These transitions could support compact frequency references and sub-micron magnetometry in a spectral region with few comparable atomic tools.
detailed_summary: This experiment observes seven magnetically induced transitions on the Cs 6S1/2 to 7P3/2 line at 456 nm that are forbidden at zero field but become prominent in applied magnetic fields. Measurements across 0.2 to 3 kG agree closely with Zeeman-Hamiltonian calculations, and the transition amplitudes can exceed those of conventional allowed lines. The transitions also exhibit large tunability, with frequency shifts up to about 17 GHz near 3 kG.
platform_or_system: Cesium vapor on the 456 nm line
main_result: The study demonstrates strong blue-wavelength magnetically induced Cs transitions with intensities above conventional lines and shifts up to roughly 17 GHz.
application_angle: The large shift and strong contrast are useful for high-resolution optical magnetometers and atomic frequency references with sub-micron spatial resolution.

title: Thermodynamic value of CHSH-induced side-information channels in a Szilard engine
abstract: We study the thermodynamic value of side-information channels induced by Bell-type correlations through a CHSH prediction task embedded into a Szilard-type feedback engine. A thermal two-level system supplies a uniformly random physical microstate $X$, and a trusted referee encoding together with a nonsignalling correlation resource induces a controller bit $G$ that acts as side information about $X$. We show that the maximal average feedback work satisfies $\langle W_{\max}\rangle \le k_B T \ln 2 , I(X:G)$, with equality achievable in the ideal quasistatic limit. For the CHSH embedding considered here, the induced channel $X \to G$ is binary symmetric with success probability $p_{\rm win}=1/2+S(P)/8$, where $S(P)$ is the CHSH value. The corresponding reversible feedback work is $k_B T \ln 2 ,[1-h_2(p_{\rm win})]$, yielding a strict ordering of the optimal classical, quantum, and nonsignalling cases. The result should be interpreted as a thermodynamic valuation of CHSH-induced side information available to the controller, not as evidence that Bell nonlocality itself is a source of free energy. The analysis assumes that the controller receives only the compressed bit $G$ and does not include the thermodynamic cost of implementing the referee, the correlation resource, or the auxiliary preprocessing. A full-cycle analysis including controller-memory reset gives non-positive net work, consistent with the second law.
one_liner: A CHSH-based side-information channel sets the reversible work extractable by a Szilard engine controller.
why_it_matters: It quantitatively links nonclassical correlation strength to information-powered thermodynamic performance in a way relevant to quantum information experiments.
detailed_summary: The paper embeds a CHSH prediction task into a Szilard-type feedback engine, where a thermal two-level system generates the microstate and a controller receives a single bit of side information. The induced channel from state to controller bit is binary symmetric with success probability determined by the CHSH value, allowing reversible feedback work to be written as kBT ln2 times the mutual information. This yields a strict ordering of classical, quantum, and nonsignalling resources while clarifying that the work value comes from accessible side information, not free energy from nonlocality itself.
platform_or_system: Two-level Szilard engine with CHSH side-information channel
main_result: The authors derive an exact work-information relation showing how CHSH-winning probability determines the maximum reversible feedback work available to the controller.
application_angle: The framework is useful for designing and interpreting quantum thermodynamics experiments that use correlated information resources for feedback and control.

title: Rethink the Role of Neural Decoders in Quantum Error Correction
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for enabling quantum advantages, with decoding as a central algorithmic primitive. Owing to its importance and intrinsic difficulty, substantial effort has been made to QEC decoder design, among which neural decoders have recently emerged as a promising data-driven paradigm. Despite this progress, practical deployment remains hindered by a fundamental accuracy-latency tradeoff, often on the microsecond timescale. To address this challenge, here we revisit neural decoders for surface-code decoding under explicit accuracy-latency constraints, considering code distances up to d=9 (161 physical qubits). We unify and redesign representative neural decoders into five architectural paradigms and develop an end-to-end compression pipeline to evaluate their deployability and performance on FPGA hardware. Through systematic experiments, we reveal several previously underexplored insights: (i) near-term decoding performance is driven more by data scale than architectural complexity; (ii) appropriate inductive bias is essential for achieving high decoding accuracy; and (iii) INT4 quantization is a prerequisite for meeting microsecond-scale latency requirements on FPGAs. Together, these findings provide concrete guidance toward scalable and real-time neural QEC decoding.
one_liner: A hardware-grounded study shows what neural QEC decoders actually need to meet microsecond FPGA latency targets.
why_it_matters: It turns neural decoding from a model-comparison exercise into deployable hardware guidance for real-time surface-code control stacks.
detailed_summary: The work benchmarks five neural-decoder architectural paradigms for surface-code decoding up to distance 9 under explicit accuracy-latency constraints, then maps them through an end-to-end compression pipeline for FPGA deployment. Systematic experiments show that decoder performance in the near term is driven more by training-data scale than architectural sophistication, while suitable inductive bias remains critical for accuracy. A central hardware conclusion is that INT4 quantization is required to satisfy microsecond-class latency on FPGA targets.
main_result: The paper shows that real-time neural surface-code decoding is feasible only with aggressive compression, especially INT4 quantization, alongside sufficient data and inductive bias.
application_angle: These results directly inform control-hardware design for fault-tolerant quantum processors where decoder latency is on the critical path.

title: Squeezing and adiabaticity breaking in time-dependent quantum harmonic oscillators
abstract: The quantum harmonic oscillator with time-dependent frequency is a paradigmatic model of driven quantum dynamics and one of the few nontrivial systems that admits an exact analytical solution. In this review paper, we present a unified treatment of the time-dependent oscillator based on the Lewis-Riesenfeld invariant method, Bogoliubov transformations and the Ermakov-Pinney equation. We show how these approaches naturally connect to squeezing for the description of excitations production, and to the breakdown of adiabaticity under generic frequency protocols. Exact results for sudden quenches and smooth ramps are discussed in detail. By explicitly bridging invariant methods and squeezing formalism, this review is meant to provide a comprehensive framework for understanding nonequilibrium dynamics in quadratic potentials, with applications ranging from thermodynamics and condensed matter to quantum control theory.
one_liner: This review unifies invariant, Bogoliubov, and Ermakov methods to analyze squeezing and adiabaticity breaking in driven oscillators.
why_it_matters: It provides a compact technical framework for designing and diagnosing squeezing protocols that recur across photonics, control, and bosonic hardware.
detailed_summary: The article presents a unified treatment of the quantum harmonic oscillator with time-dependent frequency using Lewis-Riesenfeld invariants, Bogoliubov transformations, and the Ermakov-Pinney equation. It explicitly connects excitation production and nonadiabatic dynamics to squeezing, with exact results for sudden quenches and smooth ramps. By bridging these formalisms, it clarifies how protocol shape governs adiabaticity breakdown and generated squeezing in quadratic bosonic systems.
main_result: The review consolidates exact analytic tools that map frequency-driving protocols to squeezing generation and adiabaticity breaking in quadratic quantum systems.
application_angle: The framework is practically relevant for engineering squeezing and nonadiabatic control in photonic, optomechanical, and other bosonic quantum platforms.

title: Quantum teleportation with coherent error in Bell-state measurement
abstract: Quantum teleportation is a fundamental protocol in quantum information science, whose performance is conventionally evaluated under the assumption of ideal Bell-state measurements. In realistic implementations, however, joint measurements are often imperfect and can deviate from maximally entangled bases due to coherent errors in entangling operations. In this work, we analytically show how the entanglement of joint measurements determines teleportation performance and propose a strategy to overcome the limitations imposed by partially entangled joint measurements to recover the unit teleportation fidelity. We then derive an exact equation revealing a quantitative relation between measurement entanglement, channel entanglement, and the success probability to realize the unit-fidelity teleportation. We illustrate our results using elegant joint measurements and realistic coherent error models arising from imperfect entangling operations in quantum systems. Our work provides fundamental insight into the role of measurement entanglement in quantum teleportation and establishes a practical framework for achieving faithful teleportation without requiring substantial modifications to existing hardware.
one_liner: Shows how coherent Bell-measurement errors limit teleportation and how to recover unit-fidelity teleportation probabilistically.
why_it_matters: It turns imperfect joint measurements into a usable resource model for high-fidelity teleportation without major hardware changes.
detailed_summary: The paper analytically characterizes teleportation when the Bell-state measurement is not maximally entangled because of coherent entangling-gate errors. It derives an exact relation linking measurement entanglement, channel entanglement, and the success probability for unit-fidelity teleportation. The authors also propose a strategy that compensates the limitations of partially entangled joint measurements, and illustrate it with elegant joint measurements and realistic coherent-error models.
platform_or_system: quantum teleportation with imperfect Bell-state measurements
main_result: An exact quantitative condition is derived under which partially entangled joint measurements can still enable unit-fidelity teleportation with a calculable success probability.
application_angle: This gives quantum-network implementations a practical path to faithful teleportation under realistic gate imperfections rather than requiring fully redesigned hardware.

title: Understanding oxide-thickness-dependent variability in dense Si-MOS quantum dot arrays
abstract: Achieving uniform and scalable control of semiconductor spin qubits remains a key challenge for large scale quantum computing. In this work, we investigate how gate oxide thickness influences uniformity in dense two dimensional silicon quantum dot arrays. Using a 7 x 7 array fabricated in a 300 mm CMOS-process patterned by EUV lithography, we statistically characterize 392 quantum dots across four different oxide thicknesses. The threshold voltages, capacitances, lever arms, and charging energies are extracted using parallel row based measurements and we identify an optimal SiO2 thickness of 17 nm that minimizes threshold voltage variability below 63 mV standard deviation. Our observations illustrate how multiple sources of disorder can introduce competing oxide-thickness dependencies, resulting in non-monotonic trends. These results provide key design guidelines for dense, scalable silicon spin qubit architectures.
one_liner: A 7x7 CMOS silicon quantum-dot array study identifies 17 nm oxide as the best thickness for minimizing device variability.
why_it_matters: Process-level guidance on oxide thickness directly supports more uniform, scalable silicon spin-qubit arrays.
detailed_summary: Using a 300 mm CMOS process and EUV lithography, the authors statistically analyze 392 quantum dots in a 7 x 7 silicon array across four oxide thicknesses. They extract threshold voltages, capacitances, lever arms, and charging energies using parallel row-based measurements. The data show a non-monotonic oxide-thickness dependence driven by competing disorder sources, with 17 nm SiO2 giving the lowest threshold-voltage spread below 63 mV standard deviation.
main_result: The study experimentally identifies 17 nm gate oxide as the optimum among tested values for minimizing variability in dense silicon quantum-dot arrays.
application_angle: These results provide concrete fabrication targets for building larger silicon spin-qubit processors with tighter tuning margins and better yield.

title: Optimal State Preparation for Impulse Estimation in Gaussian Quantum Systems
abstract: We present an optimal control-based strategy to enhance the estimation of impulse-like disturbances in continuously monitored linear classical and quantum systems by exploiting non-equilibrium states. Using optimal estimation techniques for linear Gaussian systems to collect information from the temporal vicinity of the disturbance, we cast the minimization of disturbance estimation uncertainty as a nonlinear optimal control problem over time-dependent system parameters. The resulting method dynamically shapes the estimation covariances through parametric modulation, maximizing information gain at a known impulse time. This differs fundamentally from conventional squeezing protocols using periodic modulation that effectively degrade inference of impulse-like disturbances. Applied to nanomechanical resonators and levitated nanoparticles, optimal parametric driving reduces estimation variance by up to a factor of two relative to steady-state operation
one_liner: Optimal parametric control of Gaussian systems halves uncertainty for estimating known-time impulse disturbances.
why_it_matters: It shows non-equilibrium state engineering can materially improve force and impulse sensing beyond steady-state squeezing strategies.
detailed_summary: The work formulates impulse-estimation enhancement in continuously monitored linear classical and quantum Gaussian systems as a nonlinear optimal-control problem over time-dependent parameters. Instead of periodic squeezing, the method shapes estimation covariances around the known disturbance time to maximize information gain. Applied to nanomechanical resonators and levitated nanoparticles, the optimized driving reduces estimation variance by up to a factor of two relative to steady-state operation.
main_result: Optimal non-equilibrium state preparation is shown to reduce impulse-estimation variance by up to 2x in representative nanomechanical and levitated platforms.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to precision force sensing and transient-event detection where the disturbance timing is known or externally triggered.

title: Probing charge noise in bilayer graphene quantum dots by Landau-Zener-Stückelberg-Majorana spectroscopy
abstract: Charge noise is an important factor limiting qubit coherence and relaxation in solid-state devices. In bilayer graphene (BLG) quantum dots, recently established as a promising platform for spin- and valley-based qubits, both the origin and magnitude of charge noise remain largely unexplored. Here, we investigate high-frequency charge noise using Landau-Zener-Stückelberg-Majorana (LZSM) interference spectroscopy. We study a single-particle charge qubit formed in a BLG double quantum dot at frequencies between 5 and 10 GHz and extract a noise spectral density $S_\varepsilon$ on the order of 0.5-0.9 neV$/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$. This is comparable to values reported for III-V semiconductor platforms and silicon. From the temperature and frequency dependence of the charge qubit decoherence, we conclude that thermal (Johnson) noise or electron-phonon coupling dominates over two-level fluctuators.
one_liner: LZSM spectroscopy quantifies high-frequency charge noise in bilayer-graphene double quantum dots at 5-10 GHz.
why_it_matters: It provides one of the first concrete noise benchmarks for bilayer-graphene qubits, a key step for assessing coherence prospects.
detailed_summary: The authors form a single-particle charge qubit in a bilayer-graphene double quantum dot and probe it with Landau-Zener-Stuckelberg-Majorana interference. From measurements between 5 and 10 GHz, they extract a charge-noise spectral density of about 0.5-0.9 neV/sqrt(Hz). Temperature and frequency trends indicate that thermal Johnson noise or electron-phonon coupling, rather than two-level fluctuators, dominate decoherence in this regime.
platform_or_system: bilayer graphene double quantum dot charge qubit
main_result: The experiment measures high-frequency charge noise in bilayer graphene at levels comparable to silicon and III-V platforms and identifies the likely dominant decoherence channels.
application_angle: These benchmarks inform materials, filtering, and operating-point choices for developing bilayer-graphene spin and valley qubits into viable hardware.

title: Cryogenic Systems for Quantum Photonic Technologies: A Practical Review
abstract: While nonclassical light sources are fundamental to quantum communication and computing, solid-state platforms like color centers and quantum dots require cryogenic temperatures to reach the performance levels necessary for practical applications. Over the past decade, low-temperature engineering has transitioned from manual handling of liquid cryogens to automated closed-cycle cryostats. This review details the principles behind modern cooling hardware ranging from flow cryostats to mechanical cryocoolers and dilution refrigerators, with a specific focus on the requirements of optical quantum devices. Aimed at the practicing scientist, this overview provides the technical insights and historical context needed to navigate the current cryogenic landscape and evaluate its role in the future of quantum technology deployment.
one_liner: A practical review maps cryostat options and engineering tradeoffs for deployable quantum photonic systems.
why_it_matters: Cryogenic packaging is a real deployment bottleneck for solid-state quantum photonics, so system-level guidance has immediate practical value.
detailed_summary: This review surveys cooling architectures spanning flow cryostats, closed-cycle mechanical cryocoolers, and dilution refrigerators, with emphasis on optical quantum-device requirements. It connects cryogenic principles to the needs of solid-state emitters such as color centers and quantum dots, including stability, automation, and integration constraints. The focus is practical rather than purely theoretical, aimed at helping experimentalists choose infrastructure for communication and computing systems.
platform_or_system: cryogenic infrastructure for solid-state quantum photonics
main_result: The paper consolidates the operating principles and engineering tradeoffs of modern cryogenic platforms for quantum photonic hardware.
application_angle: It is useful for labs and product teams selecting cooling hardware for single-photon sources and other cryogenic optical quantum technologies.

title: Transmission of signals in the 300 GHz band with a bit-error rate below ${10}^{-9}$ using a soliton comb
abstract: To address the increasing demand for ultra-high-capacity wireless communication, terahertz (THz) frequencies near 300 GHz are attracting attention as a new spectral frontier. This work presents the first experimental demonstration of error-free (BER $< 1\times10^{-9}$) 10 Gbps transmission in the 300 GHz band using a soliton microcomb generated in an integrated silicon nitride (SiN) microring resonator. While many previous microcomb-based THz demonstrations have focused on coherent modulation formats and operation near the forward-error-correction (FEC) limit, this work investigates a simple intensity-modulation/direct-detection (IM-DD) on-off keying (OOK) architecture suitable for low-complexity THz links and fiber-wireless integrated systems. Although the experiment was conducted in a short back-to-back waveguide configuration, the generated THz wave enabled stable low-BER transmission without FEC or advanced offline signal processing. Analysis of the error-free threshold power indicates the feasibility of free-space transmission over several tens of meters with high-gain antennas and THz-band amplifiers. These results demonstrate the feasibility of robust low-complexity THz photonic links based on soliton microcombs for short-range fiber-wireless integrated systems.
one_liner: Demonstrates error-free 10 Gbps transmission near 300 GHz using a SiN soliton microcomb and simple IM-DD OOK.
why_it_matters: It pushes microcomb-based THz links toward low-complexity practical wireless systems instead of DSP-heavy lab demonstrations.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a soliton microcomb generated in an integrated silicon nitride microring resonator to enable transmission in the 300 GHz band. Unlike many earlier demonstrations centered on coherent formats near FEC thresholds, this work uses low-complexity intensity modulation/direct detection with on-off keying. In a short back-to-back waveguide configuration, it achieves 10 Gbps with BER below 1e-9 without FEC or advanced offline processing, and the threshold-power analysis suggests feasibility for free-space links over tens of meters with appropriate antennas and amplifiers.
platform_or_system: SiN soliton microcomb THz photonic link
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate 10 Gbps 300 GHz transmission with BER below 10^-9 using a soliton microcomb in a simple IM-DD OOK architecture.
application_angle: This supports practical short-range fiber-wireless integration and future THz links that prioritize lower hardware and DSP complexity.

title: Lower overhead fault-tolerant building blocks for noisy quantum computers
abstract: Quantum computation holds the promise of solving certain complex problems exponentially faster than classical computers. However, the high prevalent noise in current quantum devices impedes the accurate execution of even basic algorithms. This can be remedied by protecting quantum information with a quantum error-correcting code, where the logical information of an algorithmic qubit is spread across multiple physical qubits. Individual quantum errors are then located and corrected by the fault-tolerant measurement of multi-qubit stabilizer operators (parity checks). Unfortunately, error correction and fault tolerance both impose large demands on the qubit overhead: hundreds to thousands of physical qubits per logical qubit. We reduce the spacetime cost of fault tolerance by redesigning key building blocks of an error-corrected quantum computer. First, we develop a combinatorial proof with flag fault tolerance that exponentially reduces the extra qubits needed to measure a stabilizer of any size, while tolerating one fault. We leverage these proofs to then design state preparation circuits for the Steane and Golay codes with 100% yield. Next, we improve error correction on a planar layout by showing that a distance-four code encoding six logical qubits protects information as well as the distance-five surface code, using one-tenth as many physical qubits. Finally, we optimize the time overhead of logical gates in surface code quantum computers by protecting measurement results with a classical code, cutting computation time by a factor of two to six. Our hardware-agnostic optimizations of fault tolerance overheads thus suggest new routes to advance the timeline of error-free quantum computing.
one_liner: A set of fault-tolerance primitives cuts qubit and time overheads across stabilizer readout, state prep, and logical operations.
why_it_matters: Lowering fault-tolerance overhead directly improves the feasibility and timeline of useful error-corrected quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper redesigns several core fault-tolerant building blocks in a hardware-agnostic way. It gives a combinatorial flag-fault-tolerance proof that exponentially reduces ancilla overhead for measuring stabilizers of arbitrary weight while remaining single-fault tolerant, and uses this to build 100%-yield state-preparation circuits for Steane and Golay codes. It also shows that a distance-four planar code encoding six logical qubits can match the protection of a distance-five surface code with roughly one-tenth the physical qubits, and that protecting measurement outcomes with a classical code can reduce surface-code logical gate time by about 2x to 6x.
main_result: The work presents lower-overhead fault-tolerant measurement, state-preparation, coding, and gate-scheduling methods that substantially reduce spacetime cost.
application_angle: These optimizations could make early fault-tolerant quantum hardware practical sooner by shrinking both physical-qubit requirements and runtime overhead.

title: Sub-shot-noise emission statistics of a CW-excited single photon source
abstract: Shot noise sets a fundamental limit on the sensitivity of classical optical measurements, with coherent emitters achieving the lowest possible shot-noise level. Emission from sub-Poissonian light provides a pathway to surpass this limit, and single-photon sources provide a natural platform for generating such light. However, it is commonly assumed that continuously excited single-photon sources exhibit Poissonian statistics. In this work, a theoretical model of a continuously driven two-level single-photon source is developed, treating both excitation and radiative decay as stochastic processes. The analysis demonstrates that photon emission can display sub-Poissonian statistics when excitation and decay rates are comparable, showing that continuous excitation does not inherently preclude nonclassical emission. The model is further extended to include finite detection efficiency and detector dead time, illustrating how these practical non-idealities can affect the experimental observation of sub-Poissonian statistics.
one_liner: A CW-driven two-level single-photon source can emit sub-Poissonian light when excitation and decay rates are comparable.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic route to sub-shot-noise optical signals from continuously operated single-photon emitters, relevant for metrology and source design.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a stochastic model for a continuously driven two-level emitter in which both pumping and radiative decay are treated probabilistically. Contrary to the common assumption that CW excitation implies Poissonian emission, they find sub-Poissonian photon statistics when the excitation and decay rates are of similar magnitude. The analysis is extended to finite detection efficiency and detector dead time, showing how nonideal detection reshapes the observed statistics and the conditions under which nonclassical emission remains visible.
main_result: The main result is that continuous excitation does not preclude sub-shot-noise emission statistics from a single-photon source.
application_angle: The model provides operating and detection guidelines for building continuously running nonclassical light sources for precision optical measurements.

title: QuPort: Topology-, Port-, and Congestion-Aware Compilation for Modular Multi-QPU Quantum Systems
abstract: Modular quantum processors require a compiler to reason about two resources at the same time: local device connectivity and communication across QPUs. A mapping that is acceptable on a single coupling graph may be unsuitable for a modular machine if it creates excessive cross-QPU traffic, concentrates that traffic on a small number of interconnect links, or assigns many boundary qubits to a QPU with few communication ports. This paper presents QuPort, a Python and Qiskit-based compilation framework that studies this setting through an explicit three-level model: a weighted logical interaction graph, a directed physical coupling map, and an undirected QPU-level interconnect graph. The main partitioning method, TPCCAP, optimizes the implemented objective formed by weighted cut distance, communication-port overflow, and routed link-load congestion. The framework also includes heavy-edge clustering, balanced greedy partitioning, simulated-annealing refinement, communication-port-aware layout, extraction of remote two-qubit operations, local-only routing of per-QPU circuits, and topology-aware schedule estimation. The model is a compiler-level abstraction. It does not claim a calibrated hardware runtime or an implementation of a physical remote-gate protocol.
one_liner: QuPort compiles circuits for modular multi-QPU systems by jointly optimizing topology, port limits, and interconnect congestion.
why_it_matters: Compiler-level control of cross-QPU traffic is essential for scalable modular quantum computing, where communication is a first-class bottleneck.
detailed_summary: QuPort is a Python/Qiskit compilation framework for modular quantum processors that explicitly models a weighted logical interaction graph, a directed intra-QPU coupling map, and an inter-QPU connectivity graph. Its TPCCAP partitioner optimizes an objective combining weighted cut distance, communication-port overflow, and routed link congestion, then couples this with port-aware layout, remote two-qubit operation extraction, local routing, and schedule estimation. The work is intentionally a compiler abstraction rather than a calibrated runtime study, but it exposes the resource tradeoffs unique to distributed quantum machines.
main_result: The framework demonstrates topology-, port-, and congestion-aware compilation for partitioning and scheduling circuits on modular quantum systems.
application_angle: It offers a practical software stack for mapping workloads onto networked quantum processors where communication resources are scarce and uneven.

title: Entangling Superconducting Qubits via Energy-Selective Local Reservoirs
abstract: Engineered dissipation provides a powerful route to controlling and stabilizing quantum states in open systems. Superconducting circuits are particularly suited to this approach due to their tunable coupling to dissipative environments. Here we realize programmable local reservoirs for superconducting qubits through parametrically driven coupling to readout resonators, creating energy-selective incoherent pump and loss. Using coupled superconducting qubits, we autonomously stabilize entangled single-excitation states with fidelity up to 90.8%. We probe the stabilization dynamics under varying initial conditions and bath parameters, and implement robust classical shadow estimation for accurate and scalable state characterization. Finally, we numerically study a configuration where the engineered pump and loss share a common dissipative mode, leading to reservoir-mediated interference and classically correlated steady states. Our results demonstrate a scalable and hardware-efficient framework for dissipative preparation and control of correlated many-body states in superconducting circuits.
one_liner: Programmable local reservoirs autonomously stabilize entangled superconducting-qubit states with up to 90.8% fidelity.
why_it_matters: Dissipative entanglement generation reduces active control overhead and offers a scalable route to robust state preparation in superconducting hardware.
detailed_summary: The experiment realizes local engineered reservoirs for superconducting qubits by parametrically coupling each qubit to a readout resonator, producing energy-selective incoherent pump and loss channels. Using coupled qubits, the authors autonomously stabilize single-excitation entangled states with fidelities up to 90.8% and characterize the dynamics versus initial state and bath parameters. They also apply robust classical-shadow tomography for scalable characterization and numerically study a shared-dissipator geometry where reservoir-mediated interference creates classically correlated steady states.
platform_or_system: superconducting qubits with driven readout-resonator reservoirs
main_result: The demonstrated capability is autonomous dissipative preparation and stabilization of entangled superconducting-qubit states at high fidelity.
application_angle: This reservoir-engineering approach can simplify quantum-state initialization and stabilization in larger superconducting processors without heavy feedback control.

title: Programmable Superradiance in an Interacting Qubit Array
abstract: When multiple quantum emitters couple to a common electromagnetic environment, interference in their collective radiative dynamics gives rise to superradiance and subradiance. In regimes where coherent interactions and collective dissipation compete, the microscopic many-body dynamics and quantum correlations among the emitters that underlie superradiance and subradiance are theoretically challenging and remain experimentally elusive, even though collective emission has been observed in many physical systems. Here, we realize a superconducting qubit array coupled to a common microwave waveguide that mediates collective dissipation, with simultaneous access to coherent interactions and microscopic measurements of many-body dynamics. Engineered qubit-waveguide couplings with tunable amplitude and phase enable control of collective interference and the resulting super- and subradiant states. Leveraging site-resolved control and readout, we directly observe the microscopic decay dynamics of multi-qubit states across different excitation manifolds and track the evolution of populations and tunable quantum correlations. We reveal collective decay in regimes beyond the ideal Dicke model, where strong qubit-qubit interactions stabilize superradiance and subradiance against local dephasing and reshape decay pathways through spatially and spectrally structured many-body eigenstates. Our results establish a flexible platform for exploring collective phenomena in many-body quantum optics and driven-dissipative approaches to robust quantum information processing.
one_liner: A superconducting qubit array in a common waveguide shows programmable superradiant and subradiant many-body decay.
why_it_matters: Controlling collective emission and protected dark states is valuable for microwave photonics, dissipative engineering, and resilient quantum information storage.
detailed_summary: The work uses a superconducting qubit array coupled to a common microwave waveguide, with engineered qubit-waveguide couplings whose amplitude and phase can be tuned to control collective interference. Site-resolved control and readout allow direct observation of microscopic decay dynamics across multiple excitation manifolds, including the evolution of populations and quantum correlations. Beyond ideal Dicke physics, the experiment shows that strong qubit-qubit interactions can stabilize superradiant and subradiant behavior against local dephasing and restructure decay pathways through spatially and spectrally structured eigenstates.
platform_or_system: superconducting qubit array in a microwave waveguide
main_result: The platform demonstrates programmable control and microscopic observation of collective superradiant and subradiant emission in an interacting qubit array.
application_angle: It provides a hardware testbed for collective light-matter effects that could be harnessed for protected states, microwave quantum networks, and dissipative quantum protocols.

title: Optical detection of the electron spin resonances of G centers in silicon
abstract: Color centers in silicon are emerging as promising platforms for quantum technologies. Among them, the G center has attracted considerable interest owing to its bright telecom O-band single-photon emission and its optically addressable metastable electron-spin triplet state. Here we investigate the spin properties of ensembles of G centers under above-band-gap excitation. We elucidate the spin photo-dynamics giving rise to the optical detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) response of G centers. The optimal pulsed sequence for measuring the ODMR spectrum of the G defects is identified, along with the temperature and optical-power regimes maximizing the spin readout contrast. Through magneto-optical measurements, we detect a level-anticrossing of the G center electron spin states. At last, we demonstrate coherent spin control of the defects, and characterize their spin-coherence properties. Unveiling the spin degree of freedom of the G center opens new avenues for the realization of quantum memories and quantum registers based on silicon color centers.
one_liner: Silicon G centers show optically detected spin resonance, coherent spin control, and characterized spin coherence under telecom-compatible emission.
why_it_matters: This moves a bright silicon telecom color center closer to quantum memory, register, and spin-photon interface applications.
detailed_summary: The paper studies ensembles of G centers in silicon under above-band-gap excitation and clarifies the spin photodynamics responsible for the ODMR response. It identifies pulsed sequences and temperature and optical-power regimes that maximize ODMR contrast, and uses magneto-optical measurements to detect a level anticrossing of the electron-spin states. The authors further demonstrate coherent spin control and measure the associated spin-coherence properties, establishing direct access to the center's spin degree of freedom.
main_result: The main result is optical detection and coherent control of G-center electron spins, together with optimized readout conditions and coherence characterization.
application_angle: Because G centers are silicon-based and emit in the telecom O-band, they are promising for integrated quantum memories, registers, and photonic interconnects.

title: Coherent control of spinmons
abstract: The protection of superconducting qubits from certain noise sources often comes at the cost of increased sensitivity to other decoherence channels. Here, we explore a route to avoid this tradeoff by encoding quantum information in quantum states of a transmon entangled with the spin of a trapped Andreev quasiparticle. We term such devices spinmons. We lift the spinmon Kramers degeneracy by introducing a Zeeman field and develop two routes for full qubit control via electrostatic gates and an AC flux drive, providing multiple directions for experimental implementations. Finally, we compute coherence times and verify the qubit robustness against flux and charge noise sources.
one_liner: Introduces spinmons, a hybrid qubit encoding that couples a transmon to a trapped Andreev quasiparticle spin for coherent control and noise robustness.
why_it_matters: It proposes a superconducting qubit design that may relax common noise-protection tradeoffs while retaining practical gate-control pathways.
detailed_summary: The paper encodes quantum information in states of a transmon entangled with the spin of a trapped Andreev quasiparticle, defining a new hybrid device termed a spinmon. By lifting the Kramers degeneracy with a Zeeman field, the authors develop two control routes: electrostatic gate control and AC-flux-driven control. They analyze coherence and show robustness against both flux noise and charge noise within the proposed operating regime.
platform_or_system: transmon coupled to a trapped Andreev quasiparticle spin
main_result: The work presents a fully controllable spinmon qubit architecture and calculates coherence properties indicating resilience to flux and charge noise.
application_angle: If realized experimentally, spinmons could expand the superconducting-qubit design space toward longer-lived and more tunable hardware for scalable processors.

title: Insights into the Nature of Quantum Emitters in Electron-Irradiated hexagonal Boron Nitride
abstract: Quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) have emerged as a promising solid-state platform for quantum technology applications. However, a persistent challenge in the field is the unclear origin of many observed emission lines, particularly in the visible range, which can be difficult to distinguish from signals arising from organic or process-induced contamination during sample preparations and handling. This ambiguity limits both the reproducibility of emitter generation and the reliable identification of truly intrinsic quantum defects. This work provides a step-by-step framework to assess whether quantum emitters in electron-irradiated hBN are associated with organic contaminants introduced during sample preparation. We employ hyperspectral imaging, thermal annealing, and oxygen plasma etching to investigate the origin of the green-yellow emitters in electron-irradiated hBN. The combined results not only rule out organic contamination as the source of emission but also provide insight into the spectral variability, thermal stability, and vertical localization of the emitters generated in electron-irradiated hBN that was created without any pre- or post-processing. In addition, our experiments demonstrate the feasibility of creating stable emitters in hBN with thicknesses below 10 nm. These findings provide practical guidance for the identification and controlled implementation of hBN-based single-photon emitters in quantum photonic devices.
one_liner: A stepwise study shows green-yellow quantum emitters in electron-irradiated hBN are intrinsic defects rather than organic contamination.
why_it_matters: Reliable identification of true hBN emitters is essential for reproducible single-photon-source fabrication in integrated quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: Using hyperspectral imaging, thermal annealing, and oxygen plasma etching, the authors test whether visible emitters in electron-irradiated hBN originate from process-induced organic residues. The combined evidence rules out contamination and instead supports an intrinsic defect-based origin for the green-yellow emission. They also characterize spectral variability, thermal stability, and vertical localization, and demonstrate stable emitters in flakes thinner than 10 nm.
main_result: The study establishes that the observed green-yellow emitters in electron-irradiated hBN are not caused by organic contamination and remain stable even in sub-10-nm material.
application_angle: This gives fabrication and screening guidance for building reproducible hBN single-photon emitters compatible with thin-film quantum photonic devices.

title: Controllable Quantum Memory Capacity in Quantum Reservoir Networks with Tunable partial-SWAPs
abstract: In the field of quantum reservoir computing (QRC), many different computational models and architectures have been proposed. From these models, we identify feedback-based models -- which use a feedback mechanism to re-embed classical measurements from the QRC -- and recurrent models -- which use a multi-register approach with memory and readout qubits -- as the two major competing architectures that have been discussed and validated on hardware. In this paper, we advance upon the recurrent architectures, which employ a two register approach to endow the QRC with a fading memory. While these approaches have been validated on hardware and have demonstrated great real-world performance on noisy-intermediate-scale-quantum (NISQ) quantum processing units (QPUs), the exact mechanism through which the memory capacity arises is not completely understood or fully controllable.
