The Digital Humanities community has a stake in building familiarity with this hardware now: the engineered-corpus design choices made today fix the benchmark distribution future hardware will be measured against.
one_liner: Natural and engineered text graphs are mapped onto a neutral-atom processor to benchmark graph-structured optimization on up to 100 atoms.
why_it_matters: It provides an application-layer benchmark suite for neutral-atom hardware using natively embeddable graph instances rather than abstract toy problems.
detailed_summary: The paper maps books to graphs where textual units become vertices and edges encode blockade constraints, then runs these instances on Pasqal's FRESNEL neutral-atom device. It introduces a rigidity metric to quantify how uniquely a text's structural backbone is determined and also inverts the workflow by writing texts whose graph structure matches hardware-native unit-disk layouts. Engineered instances up to 100 atoms achieve high approximation ratios, with the cleanest cases recovering the exact backbone. The resulting corpus forms a benchmark distribution for tracking neutral-atom optimization performance as devices scale.
main_result: The authors demonstrate graph-structured text instances on a cloud-accessible neutral-atom processor up to 100 atoms, with engineered cases yielding exact or near-exact backbones.
application_angle: The work creates a practical, domain-grounded benchmark and workload class for evaluating scaling behavior of neutral-atom quantum optimization hardware.

title: Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.
one_liner: Entanglement-enabled quantum MARL reaches the CHSH quantum limit and improves cooperative control beyond classical baselines.
why_it_matters: It cleanly separates true entanglement-driven advantage from generic quantum-model effects in a benchmark with a known classical ceiling.
detailed_summary: The study evaluates decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning with variational quantum-circuit actors that share entangled states. In the CHSH game, the entangled agents approach the Tsirelson bound of 0.854, while unentangled quantum circuits remain at the classical 0.75 ceiling, isolating entanglement as the active resource. On cooperative navigation, quantum actors without entanglement still substantially outperform a classical MAA2C baseline, and a hybrid setup with quantum actors plus a classical centralized critic performs best overall. The paper also shows that specific Bell-state choices can help or hurt coordination depending on the task structure.
main_result: Entangled quantum agents empirically attain near-Tsirelson CHSH performance and hybrid quantum-classical MARL improves cooperative navigation success rates over classical baselines.
application_angle: It points to concrete coordination tasks where quantum resources could become useful primitives for near-term hybrid control and decision systems.

title: Power sensitivity of broadband radiofrequency detectors based on quantum diamond spins
abstract: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centres in diamond can be used to detect radiofrequency (RF) signals through coupling of the RF magnetic field with the NV spins, combined with optical readout of the spin state. The sensitivity of such RF detectors has so far been mainly studied in terms of magnetic field sensitivity, which is relevant when the RF signal is generated by a near-field source. However, for applications where the RF input is delivered externally, a more relevant quantity is the sensitivity in terms of the input RF power. Here we theoretically analyse the power sensitivity of NV-based RF detectors as a function of the RF-spin interface geometry. We derive scaling laws of the power sensitivity for both slope-detection and variance-detection RF sensing protocols, and for various noise regimes. We find that, in most scenarios, the power sensitivity scales inversely with the characteristic physical dimension of the RF-spin interface, for instance the width of a coplanar waveguide or the diameter of a loop antenna. In other words, the smaller the structure and the probed NV volume, the better the power sensitivity, which is contrary to the case of magnetic field sensitivity. Lastly, we numerically estimate that photon shot noise limited sensitivities of 10^{-20} W Hz^{-1} (slope) and 10^{-12} W Hz^{-1/2} (variance) are achievable. This work lays the groundwork for further optimisation of NV-based RF detectors.
one_liner: This work derives how NV-diamond RF detector power sensitivity scales with antenna geometry and readout protocol.
why_it_matters: It reframes NV-based RF sensing around input power rather than field amplitude, which is the relevant metric for many real receiver architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper theoretically studies RF detection with NV centers using both slope-detection and variance-detection protocols, focusing on sensitivity expressed in input RF power. It derives scaling laws across multiple noise regimes and shows that power sensitivity usually improves inversely with the characteristic size of the RF-spin interface, such as waveguide width or loop-antenna diameter. This behavior is opposite to standard magnetic-field sensitivity scaling and favors smaller structures with smaller sensed NV volumes. Numerical estimates suggest photon-shot-noise-limited sensitivities as low as 10^-20 W Hz^-1 for slope detection and 10^-12 W Hz^-1/2 for variance detection.
main_result: The authors show that NV-based RF power sensitivity generally scales inversely with RF-spin interface size and can reach extremely low shot-noise-limited power thresholds.
application_angle: These scaling rules directly inform compact quantum RF receiver design for low-power detection in communications, spectroscopy, and near-chip sensing.

title: Multi-mode Photonic Time Crystals Based on Time-Modulated Metasurface Waveguides
abstract: Photonic time crystals are electromagnetic media with periodically time-varying parameters, enabling momentum band gaps, parametric amplification, and frequency conversion beyond what is possible in time-invariant systems. So far, they have been explored mainly in single-mode systems, which limits the range of accessible physical phenomena. Here, we introduce an impenetrable metasurface waveguide as a multimode time-varying platform supporting both guided surface modes and higher-order guided volume modes. We show that temporal modulation in this platform gives rise not only to conventional intramodal band gaps associated with same-branch coupling, but also to tilted intermodal band gaps originating from coupling between different guided-mode branches. Unlike intramodal band gaps, these intermodal band gaps are not restricted to half the modulation frequency and can enable directional wave amplification, where the amplified field carries energy along the waveguide even inside the band gap. We further show that the modulation phase difference provides an effective symmetry-control parameter: by exploiting temporal glide symmetry, one can selectively suppress or enhance gap opening for interactions between modes of the same or different symmetry. These results establish a versatile multimode platform for photonic time crystals, offering one of the simplest and most experimentally accessible routes to tilted band gaps compared with volumetric dispersive PTC implementations and, more broadly, opening new opportunities for time-varying electromagnetic systems.
one_liner: A time-modulated metasurface waveguide realizes multimode photonic time crystals with intermodal tilted band gaps and directional gain.
why_it_matters: It provides an experimentally accessible route to richer time-crystal wave physics than single-mode platforms, including symmetry-tunable amplification and mode conversion.
detailed_summary: The authors propose an impenetrable metasurface waveguide whose temporal modulation couples both guided surface modes and higher-order guided volume modes. Beyond conventional intramodal gaps, the multimode setting creates tilted intermodal band gaps arising from coupling between different mode branches, and these gaps are not constrained to half the modulation frequency. The platform supports directional amplification where energy is transported along the guide even inside the gap. By tuning modulation phase differences, temporal glide symmetry can selectively suppress or enhance gap opening between same-symmetry or different-symmetry mode pairs.
main_result: The work demonstrates that a multimode time-modulated metasurface waveguide can host tunable intermodal tilted band gaps and directional wave amplification.
application_angle: This could enable compact nonreciprocal-like photonic components, frequency converters, and amplifying waveguide devices based on time-varying metasurfaces.

title: Quantum optical synthesis of high-dimensional ultrafast frequency-bin qudits
abstract: Frequency modes of light are one of the most promising platforms that provide access to high-dimensional quantum states amongst different photonic degrees of freedom capable of high-dimensionality, enabling robust, error-tolerant, and scalable quantum optical information systems. We demonstrate engineering of precisely controlled two-photon high-dimensional states entangled in frequency through time-domain Fourier optical synthesis. We generate and convert a continuous broadband frequency-entangled state into a large range of discrete frequency bins suitable for ITU standards, with spacings ranging from 12.5 GHz to 750 GHz, and observe spectral anticorrelations over 38 frequency bins, including intra-bin pure states at a 100 GHz bin spacing. We characterize the full quantum state dimensionality via Schmidt decomposition and observe lower bounds on the frequency-binned Hilbert-space dimensionalities of at least 289, formed by two entangled qudits with dimension 17. Furthermore, we demonstrate quantum nonlocality via frequency correlations in a transmission experiment over a campus-scale two-node fiber network. This work represents a crucial step towards building a versatile and relatively simple way of generating precisely controlled high-dimensional spectral qudits, with the potential of harnessing in wavelength-multiplexed quantum networks, high-dimensional information processing, and communication of quantum states specifically, and fiber-optic quantum remote sensing.
one_liner: Time-domain Fourier synthesis creates telecom-compatible high-dimensional frequency-bin entangled qudits across a wide bin-spacing range.
why_it_matters: It advances fiber-ready high-dimensional photonic entanglement with large Hilbert-space size for multiplexed quantum networking and communications.
detailed_summary: The work converts a broadband frequency-entangled two-photon state into discrete ITU-compatible frequency bins using time-domain Fourier optical synthesis, covering spacings from 12.5 GHz to 750 GHz. The authors observe spectral anticorrelations across 38 bins and identify intra-bin pure states at 100 GHz spacing. Schmidt analysis gives a lower-bound Hilbert-space dimensionality of at least 289, corresponding to two entangled 17-dimensional qudits. They also demonstrate nonlocal frequency correlations after transmission over a campus-scale two-node fiber link.
platform_or_system: frequency-entangled photons in fiber-compatible frequency bins
main_result: The authors generate and distribute precisely controlled high-dimensional frequency-bin entanglement with at least 289-dimensional joint Hilbert space over a two-node fiber network.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to wavelength-multiplexed quantum networks, high-capacity quantum communication, and remote fiber-optic quantum sensing.

title: Timing Jitter Induced by Stochastic Baseline Fluctuations in High-Count-Rate Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors
abstract: Superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) have demonstrated timing jitter in the few-picosecond regime, yet their timing resolution deteriorates substantially under high-count-rate operation. Existing interpretations mainly attribute this degradation to deterministic waveform distortions, such as multiphoton responses and pulse pile-up, yet the experimentally observed jitter broadening at high count rates cannot be fully accounted for within this picture. Here, we show that stochastic baseline fluctuations arising from finite-memory readout dynamics constitute an intrinsic source of the count-rate-dependent timing jitter in SNSPD systems. For stochastically arriving photons, overlapping recovery responses accumulate in the readout chain and generate statistically fluctuating baselines, which are converted into timing uncertainty through threshold-based timing extraction. We develop a stochastic-process framework that quantitatively connects photon statistics, readout dynamics, and timing jitter. The framework predicts characteristic scaling behaviors, including a nonmonotonic dependence of baseline fluctuations under pulsed excitation with a maximum near half of the repetition frequency. These predictions are quantitatively verified through systematic variations of count rate, circuit time constant, and detector dynamical properties. Our results identify stochastic baseline dynamics as a fundamental mechanism limiting timing resolution in high-count-rate SNSPD operation and provide a general framework for optimizing finite-memory high-speed photon-counting systems.
one_liner: A stochastic model explains why SNSPD timing jitter worsens at high count rates through baseline fluctuations in finite-memory readout chains.
why_it_matters: It identifies a fundamental high-rate timing limit in SNSPD systems and provides a practical route to optimize detector-readout design for fast photon counting.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that under stochastic photon arrivals, overlapping recovery responses in finite-memory readout electronics produce random baseline shifts that map into threshold-timing uncertainty. A stochastic-process framework quantitatively links photon statistics, detector dynamics, circuit time constants, and jitter broadening. The theory predicts distinctive scaling laws, including a nonmonotonic baseline-fluctuation peak near half the repetition frequency under pulsed excitation. Experiments varying count rate, circuit time constant, and detector dynamics validate the model.
main_result: The main result is that stochastic baseline fluctuations, not just deterministic pulse distortion, fundamentally limit SNSPD timing resolution at high count rates.
application_angle: This directly informs design of high-speed quantum photonics receivers, lidar, and optical timing systems that rely on picosecond-resolved single-photon detection.

title: Toward Covert Quantum Computing
abstract: As quantum computers become available through multi-tenant cloud platforms, ensuring privacy against adversaries sharing the same quantum processing unit becomes critical. We introduce and explore \emph{covert quantum computing}, a new concept that ensures an adversary with access to all other quantum computational units (QCUs) of a quantum computer cannot detect computation on the subset that they cannot access. Analogous to covert communication, we employ information theory. However, since here the adversary controls the systems used for detection, we require a richer framework for covertness analysis that accounts for the use of quantum memories and adaptive operations. Thus, we adopt the \emph{quantum-strategy} framework used in quantum game theory and memory channel discrimination. Current quantum computers use planar graph circuit layouts and typically assume nearest-neighbor crosstalk. We derive discrete isoperimetric inequalities to show that, for an $n$-qubit circuit under this model, only $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ border qubits provide detection information to the adversary. We then explore this scaling law on IQM's 54-qubit \emph{Emerald} processor and IBM's 156-qubit \emph{ibm\_fez} machine employing the Heron 2 architecture. We implement Ramsey experiments on qubits not used in computation, and detect nearest-neighbor crosstalk, as expected. However, we also observe long-range coupling effects beyond the border qubits, revealing a side channel that the adversary can exploit. We hypothesize that this long-range crosstalk is induced by leakage from the drive and control lines. Beyond weakening covertness, it exposes co-tenants to both adversarial and unintended crosstalk and degrades circuits that span spatially distributed qubits, motivating further work on spatial isolation and crosstalk characterization.
one_liner: Covert quantum computing is formulated and tested by probing crosstalk leakage on superconducting cloud QPUs shared by multiple tenants.
why_it_matters: It exposes concrete side channels that can reveal or disturb hidden computations on shared quantum hardware, making it highly relevant to secure cloud QC deployment.
detailed_summary: The authors define covert quantum computing using a quantum-strategy framework where an adversary controls accessible quantum computational units and can use adaptive quantum memories. For planar nearest-neighbor architectures, they derive isoperimetric bounds implying only O(sqrt(n)) border qubits should leak detection information for an n-qubit circuit. They then test this on IQM's 54-qubit Emerald and IBM's 156-qubit ibm_fez processors via Ramsey measurements on spectator qubits. While nearest-neighbor crosstalk is observed as expected, they also find long-range coupling beyond border qubits, likely from control-line leakage, creating a stronger side channel.
main_result: The study shows that real multi-tenant superconducting QPUs exhibit detectable long-range crosstalk beyond nearest-neighbor boundaries, undermining covert execution assumptions.
application_angle: The results matter for secure quantum cloud services, tenant isolation, hardware benchmarking, and mitigation of crosstalk-induced performance loss in large distributed circuits.

title: Optimizing the preparation of Dicke states using counterdiabatic driving methods
abstract: Recently, the technique of counterdiabatic driving, which provides an effective strategy for accelerating adiabatic quantum evolution, has been widely applied in the preparation of many-body quantum states. In this work, we propose a theoretical scheme for the efficient preparation of Dicke states in a system of non-interacting two-level atoms. Our approach leverages the one-axis twisting (OAT) interaction to generate non-classical correlations and combines it with time-dependent external fields to achieve precise control over the dynamics of the system. By employing rapid adiabatic passage (RAP), it demonstrates how the system can be steered from an initial coherent spin state to a target Dicke state with high fidelity [S. C. Carrasco, M. H. Goerz, S. A. Malinovskaya, V. Vuletić, W. P. Schleich, and V. S. Malinovsky, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{132}, 153603 (2024)]. To further optimize the preparation process, we introduce counterdiabatic driving (CD), which suppresses non-adiabatic transitions. Numerical simulations confirm that our scheme can achieve high-fidelity Dicke states for a moderate number of particles. Our results provide a scalable and experimentally feasible approach to prepare Dicke states, with potential applications in quantum metrology, quantum communication, and quantum information processing.
one_liner: Counterdiabatic driving accelerates high-fidelity Dicke-state preparation in non-interacting atomic ensembles using OAT-assisted rapid adiabatic passage.
why_it_matters: It offers a scalable state-preparation route for entangled resources used in metrology, networking, and collective-spin quantum protocols.
detailed_summary: The scheme combines one-axis twisting with time-dependent external fields to steer a coherent spin state toward a target Dicke state in a non-interacting two-level-atom ensemble. Rapid adiabatic passage provides the baseline transfer protocol, while counterdiabatic terms suppress nonadiabatic excitations and shorten the preparation time. Numerical simulations show high-fidelity Dicke-state generation for moderate particle numbers. The method is positioned as experimentally feasible and more efficient than purely adiabatic evolution.
main_result: The main result is a numerically validated shortcut protocol that prepares Dicke states with high fidelity by augmenting RAP with counterdiabatic control.
application_angle: Dicke states are useful for enhanced quantum sensing, multipartite networking primitives, and collective-spin resources in quantum information processing.

title: Nonreciprocal magnon-magnon entanglement in a spinning cavity-magnon system
abstract: Cavity-magnon systems, combining magnons and photons, offer a versatile platform for studying quantum entanglement and advancing quantum information science. In this work, we propose a scheme for generating nonreciprocal magnon-magnon entanglement in a hybrid system consisting of two yttrium iron garnet spheres coupled to a spinning whispering-gallery-mode cavity. By leveraging the magnon Kerr nonlinearity and the Sagnac effect arising from the cavity rotation, we show that the entanglement can be substantially enhanced, and the resulting entanglement exhibits pronounced nonreciprocal characteristics. Furthermore, our scheme demonstrates that the entanglement remains robust against thermal noise and persists at bath temperatures up to 100 mK. This work underscores the potential of spinning cavity-magnon systems as a versatile platform for realizing nonreciprocal quantum devices and facilitating the development of quantum technologies.
one_liner: A rotating whispering-gallery cavity coupled to two YIG magnons can produce thermally robust nonreciprocal magnon-magnon entanglement.
why_it_matters: It points to directional hybrid quantum devices where entanglement generation can be enhanced and controlled by rotation and nonlinearity.
detailed_summary: The proposed hybrid system couples two YIG-sphere magnon modes to a spinning whispering-gallery-mode cavity, using cavity rotation to induce a Sagnac asymmetry and magnon Kerr nonlinearity to boost correlations. Within this setting, the authors predict substantially enhanced magnon-magnon entanglement with a pronounced directional, nonreciprocal character. Their analysis indicates that the entanglement persists against thermal noise up to bath temperatures of about 100 mK. The work frames spinning cavity-magnon hardware as a platform for nonreciprocal quantum functionalities.
platform_or_system: spinning WGM cavity coupled to two YIG magnons
main_result: The paper predicts enhanced and direction-dependent magnon-magnon entanglement enabled jointly by cavity rotation and magnon Kerr nonlinearity.
application_angle: Such nonreciprocal entangled hybrid devices could support directional quantum transduction, robust microwave sensing architectures, and specialized quantum network components.

title: Discrete-phase-randomized mode-pairing quantum key distribution
abstract: Mode-pairing quantum key distribution (MP-QKD) protocol achieves performance beyond the repeaterless rate-transmittance bound and exhibits excellent practicality by avoiding the requirement for difficult global phase locking. However, the source side of MP-QKD still relies on the assumption of continuous phase randomization, an experimentally infeasible requirement in practice. Therefore, the practical security of the protocol cannot be fully guaranteed. In this work, we propose a discrete-phase-randomized mode-pairing quantum key distribution (DPR-MP-QKD) protocol and analyze the basis-dependence of the source side. Then, we introduce a concrete discrete version of the decoy state method that ensures the security of the DPR-MP-QKD protocol. Finally, simulation results indicate that as the number of discrete phases increases, the key rate performance of DPR-MP-QKD progressively approaches that of the continuous case, with convergence achieved at approximately 14 discrete phases. Moreover, our approach drastically lowers the demand for randomness. While conventional continuous phase randomization demands an unlimited supply of random bits, we show that merely a few bits (e.g., 4) are adequate.
one_liner: Discrete-phase-randomized MP-QKD closes a practical security gap while retaining near-continuous-performance key rates with only a few random bits.
why_it_matters: It makes a beyond-repeaterless-style QKD protocol more experimentally realistic by replacing infeasible continuous phase randomization with a secure discrete scheme.
detailed_summary: The work reformulates mode-pairing QKD with discrete phase randomization, explicitly analyzing source basis dependence that arises when continuous randomization is unavailable in practice. The authors introduce a discrete decoy-state method to recover security for the discrete-phase protocol. Simulations show that the key rate approaches the continuous-randomization limit as the phase alphabet grows, with convergence around 14 discrete phases. They also show the randomness cost drops sharply, with roughly 4 random bits sufficient in a representative setting.
main_result: The main result is a secure discrete-phase MP-QKD protocol whose simulated key rate nearly matches the continuous-phase case with about 14 phase values.
application_angle: This improves deployability of long-distance practical QKD by reducing randomness requirements and removing an experimentally unrealistic source assumption.

title: Spin chirality across quantum state copies detects hidden entanglement
abstract: Entanglement can hide in two fundamentally different ways. First, multi-copy correlations can carry information that no single-copy measurement on an unknown state is able to access. Second, bound entangled states possess a positive partial transpose, which makes them invisible to the Peres-Horodecki criterion and all moment inequalities that depend on it. Here we show that the moment difference between the partial transpose and purity decomposes exactly as a chirality-chirality correlator, where the relevant operator is the scalar spin chirality -- the same quantity that governs chiral spin liquids and the topological Hall effect. This decomposition identifies the specific physical structure that multi-copy entanglement detection probes. Using the same controlled-SWAP circuits, we develop a multi-channel spectral classifier for bound entanglement. The classifier combines realignment spectral features with chirality corrections and achieves 99.9% recall at zero false positives across all three known 3x3 bound entangled families, compared with ~40% for the CCNR criterion alone. We also introduce a marginal-noise construction that produces CCNR-invisible bound entangled states, which the classifier detects but which remain invisible to all single-parameter criteria. We validate our approach experimentally on three IBM Quantum processors and demonstrate negativity reconstruction with mean errors of 0.002-0.027, chirality detection for pure and mixed entangled states, and bound entanglement detection across two structurally distinct families (Horodecki and chessboard) on a single gate-based superconducting processor.
one_liner: A multi-copy chirality observable on superconducting hardware exposes hidden and bound entanglement missed by standard single-copy tests.
why_it_matters: It links entanglement detection to a measurable physical correlator and greatly improves bound-entanglement identification on current gate-based processors.
detailed_summary: The work proves that the gap between partial-transpose moments and purity can be written exactly as a chirality-chirality correlator built from scalar spin chirality across state copies. Using controlled-SWAP circuits, the authors construct a spectral classifier that combines realignment features with chirality corrections to detect bound entanglement, including CCNR-invisible cases created by marginal-noise constructions. They experimentally validate negativity reconstruction, chirality detection, and bound-entanglement detection on three IBM Quantum superconducting processors with reported mean negativity errors of 0.002-0.027.
main_result: The method achieves 99.9% recall at zero false positives across the three known 3x3 bound-entangled families while running on gate-based superconducting hardware.
application_angle: This provides a practical diagnostic layer for near-term quantum processors to certify nonclassical correlations that conventional entanglement checks miss.

title: Quantum-enabled complete RF-polarimetry with an optically-wired atomic sensor
abstract: Rydberg atomic electrometry leverages the extreme sensitivity of highly excited atoms for calibration-free electric field measurements. The technique uses a non-metallic vapor cell to link properties of an RF field to a spectroscopic readout in the optical domain. Most demonstrations have so far focused on detecting linearly-polarized fields, for which the induced splitting of dressed atomic levels is rotationally invariant. Here we report on Rydberg atomic measurements of RF fields in a general state of polarization (SOP) which we map onto the Poincaré sphere through spectroscopic fingerprints. For a Stokes vector circumnavigating a Poincaré sphere meridian, we witness a continuous transformation of the atomic eigenenergy spectrum. Because the relative positions of eigenenergies are locked in place by quantization of angular momentum, the framework is universal and calibration free. We provide a specific demonstration in rubidium, which generalizes to all systems with a single valence electron.
one_liner: A Rydberg atomic sensor performs calibration-free complete RF polarimetry by mapping arbitrary field polarization onto optical spectra.
why_it_matters: Complete polarization-resolved RF sensing with an atom-based, nonmetallic probe is attractive for traceable field metrology in environments where antennas are limiting.
detailed_summary: The paper extends Rydberg atomic electrometry beyond linearly polarized fields by associating general RF states of polarization with distinct spectroscopic fingerprints in an optically read out vapor-cell sensor. As the Stokes vector traverses a meridian on the Poincare sphere, the measured dressed-state eigenenergies evolve continuously, enabling full SOP reconstruction. Because the level structure is fixed by angular-momentum quantization, the protocol is presented as universal and calibration free, with a concrete rubidium implementation.
platform_or_system: optically wired rubidium Rydberg vapor-cell sensor
main_result: The authors demonstrate complete RF polarimetry by spectroscopically resolving arbitrary polarization states and mapping them onto the Poincare sphere without conventional calibration.
application_angle: The approach could enable compact, antenna-free RF field characterization for metrology, communications diagnostics, and sensing in electromagnetically challenging settings.

title: Quantum battery optimized by parametric amplification
abstract: The parametric amplification enabled by two-photon driving constitutes a versatile platform for advanced quantum technologies. We present an optimized scheme for implementing quantum batteries (QBs) based on a superconducting circuit system, where a two-photon-driven LC resonator serves as the charger and an array of transmon qubits functions as the battery. Our results show that two-photon parametric driving exponentially enhances the effective cavity-qubit coupling, which in turn gives rise to near-degenerate energy-level structures and highly entangled quantum states. This significantly enhances the charging power and enables rapid energy transfer from the charger to the battery. Moreover, the engineered squeezed cavity mode and the associated quantum correlations effectively suppress environmentally induced decoherence, thereby delaying energy leakage and facilitating stable energy storage. The proposed scheme remains robust against practical experimental imperfections, such as parameter disorder and environmental noise, preserving its performance advantages. The work provides a feasible platform for realizing high-power, high-stability QBs and highlights the potential of parametric control in quantum energy technologies.
one_liner: Two-photon parametric driving boosts a superconducting quantum battery by amplifying cavity-qubit coupling and suppressing decoherence.
why_it_matters: It identifies an experimentally plausible control knob for faster charging and longer energy retention in superconducting quantum-energy hardware.
detailed_summary: The proposal uses a two-photon-driven LC resonator as the charger for a battery composed of multiple transmon qubits in a superconducting circuit architecture. Parametric amplification exponentially enhances effective cavity-qubit coupling, creating near-degenerate spectra and strongly entangled states that accelerate energy transfer. The squeezed cavity mode and induced correlations also reduce environmentally driven decoherence and leakage, while simulations indicate robustness against disorder and noise.
platform_or_system: two-photon-driven superconducting LC resonator with transmon array
main_result: Parametric amplification yields substantially higher charging power together with more stable stored energy than conventional superconducting quantum-battery operation.
application_angle: If realized, the scheme offers a route to practical high-power quantum energy storage modules compatible with superconducting-circuit platforms.

title: Fraxonium: Fractional fluxon states for qudit encoding
abstract: We propose a superconducting circuit hosting $d$ low-lying states, well separated from the rest of the spectrum, that naturally realizes a qudit system protected from leakage errors. The system represents a generalization of the fluxonium and the low-energy states are constituted by fractional fluxon states, that we call {\it fraxons}, localized in the minima of a suitably designed Josephson potential. The latter is tailored through a Fourier engineering approach, that employs multi-harmonic Josephson building block elements composed by a Josephson junction and an inductance connected in series. We present the spectrum of a $d=4$ and a $d=5$ qudit system and study in detail the qutrit case. We analyze the dipole matrix elements for coupling to radiation and propose a non-Abelian, stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) protocol for single-qutrit gates, that is particularly suited for the present system. The proposed platform opens novel perspectives in circuit engineering and quantum computing beyond the qubit paradigm.
one_liner: Fraxonium circuits engineer fractional fluxon states to realize leakage-protected superconducting qudits beyond the qubit paradigm.
why_it_matters: Hardware-native qudits with isolated low-lying manifolds could reduce leakage and increase encoding density in superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The authors design a generalized fluxonium circuit whose Fourier-engineered Josephson potential supports d well-separated low-energy states localized as fractional fluxons, or fraxons. They present spectra for d=4 and d=5 devices and analyze the qutrit case in detail, including dipole matrix elements for radiative coupling. A non-Abelian STIRAP protocol is proposed for single-qutrit control, leveraging the protected low-energy manifold to suppress leakage out of the computational space.
main_result: The architecture realizes a naturally isolated multi-level qudit manifold and supports a concrete adiabatic gate scheme for qutrit control.
application_angle: This could enable denser and more leakage-resilient superconducting logic using native qutits or higher-dimensional qudits instead of qubit-only encodings.

title: Entangled Telecom Photon Generation using Twisted Van der Waals Crystals
abstract: Nanoscale quantum light sources are essential building blocks for integrated quantum photonic systems. Here, we report a wavelength-scale entangled-photon source based on van der Waals-engineered NbOBr$_2$, and benchmark its performance for telecom-wavelength quantum light generation. By exploiting the material's second-order nonlinearity, we generate quantum-correlated photon pairs via spontaneous parametric down-conversion. We then use a 90$^{\circ}$ twisted stacking to induce quantum interference in photon-pair generation, yielding polarization-entangled photons. This approach enables tunability of the quantum optical state via control of the excitation laser polarization. We experimentally obtain entanglement fidelities exceeding 95% for Bell states, along with a high coincidence-to-accidental ratio of $\sim$335, and a brightness approximately one order of magnitude higher than recently reported telecom sources based on transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) 2D materials. These results establish twisted van der Waals engineering as a powerful platform for highly tunable, high-brightness quantum light sources at telecom wavelengths.
one_liner: Twisted NbOBr2 van der Waals crystals generate bright telecom Bell pairs from a wavelength-scale entangled-photon source.
why_it_matters: It shows a compact 2D-material route to high-fidelity, tunable telecom entanglement for integrated quantum photonics and networking.
detailed_summary: The source uses the second-order nonlinearity of van der Waals-engineered NbOBr2 to generate photon pairs by spontaneous parametric down-conversion at telecom wavelengths. A 90 degree twisted stacking geometry induces quantum interference between pair-generation pathways, producing polarization-entangled output with optical-state tunability via pump polarization. The experiment reports Bell-state fidelities above 95%, a coincidence-to-accidental ratio near 335, and roughly an order-of-magnitude brightness improvement over recent telecom TMD-based sources.
platform_or_system: 90 degree twisted NbOBr2 van der Waals photon source
main_result: The device demonstrates tunable telecom polarization-entangled photon generation with >95% Bell-state fidelity and high brightness from a wavelength-scale 2D-material platform.
application_angle: Its small footprint and telecom compatibility make it promising for on-chip entanglement sources in fiber-networked quantum communication systems.

title: Development of an electrodynamic balance to study single levitated particles exposed to alkali-metal vapor
abstract: Electrodynamic balances (EDBs) have been widely used to investigate reactions between levitated particles and background gases. In this paper, we report the development of an EDB that exposes trapped particles to alkali-metal vapor. The apparatus was developed principally to investigate the interactions between such vapor and the paraffin used as a spin anti-relaxation coating for alkali-metal vapor cells by atomic physicists. The trap electrodes of the EDB were installed in a vacuum glass cell. Particles were loaded via laser launching, without venting or contaminating the cell. Alkali-metal vapor was released from a dedicated dispenser. We found changes in the charge-to-mass ratios of trapped particles irradiated with ultraviolet light after exposure to alkali-metal vapor. These results demonstrate the utility of the apparatus.
one_liner: An electrodynamic balance now traps single particles while exposing them to alkali vapor, enabling controlled studies of surface-vapor interactions.
why_it_matters: It creates a needed testbed for materials used in alkali-vapor quantum devices, especially spin-preserving cell coatings.
detailed_summary: The apparatus integrates an electrodynamic balance inside a vacuum glass cell and adds an alkali-metal dispenser so single trapped particles can be studied under controlled vapor exposure. Particles are introduced by laser launching without venting or contaminating the cell, preserving the alkali environment. Initial measurements show UV-induced changes in charge-to-mass ratio after alkali exposure, demonstrating sensitivity to particle-surface modifications relevant to coating studies.
platform_or_system: electrodynamic balance with alkali-vapor exposure cell
main_result: The team built and validated an EDB platform that can levitate single particles in alkali vapor and detect exposure-induced changes in particle charge-to-mass ratio.
application_angle: This enables direct materials testing for alkali-vapor-cell components, which can improve coatings and reliability in atomic sensors and clocks.

title: Blind Quantum Computation on a Modular Superconducting Processor
abstract: Current cloud-based quantum processors offer access to advanced hardware hosted on a remote server, but do not guarantee data or algorithm privacy. Blind quantum computation provides information-theoretic privacy by enabling a client to execute an algorithm without disclosing information about either the task or the final result. Here, we execute a measurement-based blind quantum computation protocol on a superconducting processor comprising two flip-chip-bonded modules, one acting as a server and the other as a client. The server generates a two-dimensional cluster state and forwards it to the client. Using this resource, the client implements a universal gate set with only adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements. To illustrate this approach, we execute a three-qubit instance of the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm. We analyze the server's quantum state after each rotation of a measurement-based single-qubit gate to verify that negligible information about the computation is revealed to the server, consistent with the one-way flow of information that guarantees blindness. This proof-of-principle demonstration establishes key elements of blind quantum computation in superconducting-circuit architectures, indicating that intermediate-scale implementations of blind protocols may become feasible with realistic near-term improvements in gate fidelities.
one_liner: A modular superconducting chip demonstrates measurement-based blind quantum computation with privacy preserved against the server.
why_it_matters: It shows a realistic route to privacy-preserving cloud quantum computing on superconducting hardware using modular client-server separation.
detailed_summary: The experiment implements blind quantum computation on a two-module flip-chip-bonded superconducting processor, with one module preparing a 2D cluster state and the other acting as the client. The client performs adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements to realize a universal gate set in the measurement-based model. A three-qubit Deutsch-Jozsa instance is executed as a proof of principle. Tomographic analysis of the server-side state after gate steps indicates negligible leakage about the delegated computation, consistent with information-theoretic blindness.
main_result: The authors experimentally realize blind measurement-based quantum computation on a modular superconducting device and verify negligible server information gain during the computation.
application_angle: The work targets secure remote quantum computing services where users can run algorithms on cloud superconducting processors without revealing inputs, circuits, or outputs.

title: Integrated photonic computing: towards high-dimensional information processing
abstract: The rapid growth of artificial intelligence, coupled with the slowing of Moore's law, is straining computing infrastructure, as CMOS electronics face inherent limits in bandwidth, energy efficiency, and parallelism. Integrated photonic computing encodes and processes information using the phase, amplitude, spatial modes, wavelength channels, and polarisation of guided optical fields, offering a scalable and energy-efficient route beyond charge-based signalling. Here, we review on-chip photonic computing, emphasising the progression from low-dimensional to high-dimensional architectures. At the foundational level, low-dimensional approaches manipulate the phase and amplitude of guided light through Mach-Zehnder interferometers, diffractive structures, microring resonators, and absorptive elements, forming a programmable basis for optical matrix-vector multiplication. Crucially, high-dimensional architectures exploit spatial modes and wavelength channels to carry multiple independent data streams through a single waveguide, achieving higher throughput with moderate hardware overhead. Practical deployment, however, demands more than device innovation. We examine how system-level techniques, from time-wavelength interleaving to hardware-aware training, address energy efficiency, precision, and algorithm-hardware co-design. Five challenges nevertheless remain: electro-optic conversion efficiency, computing parallelism, spatial integration, reconfigurability, and robustness. We highlight emerging topological structures, such as optical skyrmions, as a promising route to fault-tolerant, topologically protected encoding that exploits the largely untapped polarisation degree of freedom. We argue that, by embracing the higher dimensionality of light, photonic computing can offer not merely an incremental improvement but a new paradigm for high-performance, energy-efficient information processing.
one_liner: This review maps how integrated photonic computing can scale from MZI-based cores to high-dimensional mode- and wavelength-multiplexed processors.
why_it_matters: It frames the device and system co-design needed for practical, energy-efficient photonic accelerators beyond conventional CMOS scaling.
detailed_summary: The paper reviews integrated photonic computing architectures, starting from low-dimensional phase- and amplitude-based processors built from interferometers, microrings, diffractive elements, and absorptive devices. It emphasizes higher-dimensional encoding using spatial modes, wavelength channels, and polarization to increase throughput per waveguide with moderate hardware overhead. System-level strategies such as time-wavelength interleaving and hardware-aware training are discussed alongside bottlenecks in electro-optic conversion, parallelism, integration density, reconfigurability, and robustness. The authors also highlight topological optical structures, including skyrmions, as a possible route to fault-tolerant information encoding.
main_result: The main contribution is a technical synthesis showing that high-dimensional integrated photonics can substantially expand throughput and efficiency relative to low-dimensional optical compute fabrics.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to optical AI and signal-processing hardware, where multiplexed on-chip photonics could deliver higher bandwidth and lower energy per operation.

title: Quantum Meets Statistical-Physical Secrecy: A Novel Hybrid Key Distribution Architecture
abstract: This letter proposes a novel hybrid key distribution architecture that jointly exploits quantum key distribution (QKD) and Kirchhoff-law-Johnson-noise (KLJN) statistical-physical key exchange. In the proposed system, an optical BB84-type QKD link operates in coordination with a parallel wired KLJN link, which is used for secure basis handling and, in selected protocols, additional raw key generation. Three novel KLJN-assisted QKD protocols are introduced to eliminate public basis disclosure messages and bit sifting, extract basis-derived key bits, or generate raw key bits under ideal KLJN assumptions. Analytical expressions for the normalized key rate and absolute throughput are derived by accounting for optical channel penalties, KLJN bandwidth constraints, and synchronization bottlenecks. Numerical results show that the proposed hybrid architecture can improve key generation efficiency and throughput in short-haul infrastructures, including metropolitan area networks (MANs) and data center interconnects.
one_liner: A hybrid BB84-KLJN architecture uses a wired noise-based channel to assist QKD and improve key efficiency in short-haul links.
why_it_matters: It proposes a practical way to reduce public-basis overhead and raise secret-key throughput in metro and data-center quantum links.
detailed_summary: The letter introduces a joint architecture combining an optical BB84-type QKD link with a parallel wired Kirchhoff-law-Johnson-noise channel. The KLJN link is used for secure basis handling and, depending on protocol choice, can also contribute raw key material. Three KLJN-assisted QKD variants are analyzed to remove public basis disclosure and bit sifting or to extract extra key bits from basis information. Analytical key-rate expressions and numerical evaluation indicate improved normalized efficiency and throughput under realistic optical loss, KLJN bandwidth, and synchronization constraints.
platform_or_system: hybrid optical QKD plus wired KLJN link
main_result: The paper proposes and analyzes three KLJN-assisted QKD protocols that can increase key-generation efficiency and throughput for short-reach deployments.
application_angle: The architecture is aimed at metropolitan networks and data-center interconnects, where parallel optical and wired infrastructure can be co-deployed to boost secure key delivery.

title: Telecom-Wavelength-Compatible Quantum Information Transcription Using Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers
abstract: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are a leading platform for solid-state quantum sensing and quantum information processing. While most optical studies rely on the visible fluorescence associated with the triplet transitions, the infrared singlet transition near $1042$ nm, which is typically considered dark within the singlet manifold of the NV optical cycle, provides an alternative optical channel. Here, we report wavelength-resolved optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) measurements of this infrared emission. We directly observe ODMR contrast in the $1042$ nm emission and analyze its dependence on the magnetic field. The field-dependent spectral dispersion of the ODMR signal demonstrates that the spin-state information encoded in the NV center is transcribed to the infrared singlet emission through the spin-selective intersystem crossing, in close analogy to the visible fluorescence readout. These results establish infrared ODMR as a high-fidelity optical readout pathway. Crucially, by extending spin-state transcription directly into the $1300-1600$ nm range, this work demonstrates a direct, conversion-free interface between diamond spin-qubits and standard telecommunication infrastructure, bypassing the efficiency bottlenecks of active frequency conversion and benefiting from the already well-developed technologies in this range of the electromagnetic spectrum.
one_liner: NV centers show ODMR contrast in 1042 nm infrared emission, opening a telecom-compatible spin readout path without frequency conversion.
why_it_matters: It creates a direct interface between diamond spin qubits and telecom-band infrastructure for sensing and networking.
detailed_summary: The authors perform wavelength-resolved ODMR on the infrared singlet transition of the NV center near 1042 nm and directly observe spin-dependent contrast in this nominally dark channel. They study how the ODMR spectrum disperses with magnetic field, showing that spin-state information is transcribed into the infrared emission via the spin-selective intersystem crossing. This establishes an alternative optical readout pathway complementary to visible fluorescence. The work further argues that the transcription can be extended directly into the 1300-1600 nm telecom window without active frequency conversion.
main_result: The study demonstrates infrared ODMR readout from NV centers and shows that spin information can be carried into telecom-compatible optical channels.
application_angle: This is useful for fiber-connected quantum sensors and spin-photon interfaces, reducing the need for lossy frequency conversion between diamond emitters and telecom networks.

title: Optimizing strong light-matter coupling of plasmonic lattices and monolayer semiconductors
abstract: Exciton-polaritons provide a versatile platform for the study of a wide range of phenomena, including polariton lasers, topological polaritons, and bosonic condensation. Transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers host excitons with large oscillator strength and binding energies constituting a robust matter constituent that forms polaritons from cryogenic to room temperature when embedded in optical microcavities. Plasmonic nanoparticles arranged in lattice geometries offer strong field-confinement and high quality factors. However, the high sensitivity of monolayer excitons to strain and dielectric disorder necessitates encapsulation in atomically flat hBN to ensure a high optical quality, rendering plasmonics more challenging. Here, we employ our recently developed fabrication method for embedding gold nanodisk arrays into van der Waals heterostructures and compare two samples with opposite layer order. We observe that strain and etching-induced surface contamination can reduce the exciton quality and thus the light-matter interaction strength significantly. Our fabrication approach reduces interfacial irregularities and enables homogeneous large-area polariton lattices for a wide range of applications, such as polarization-control or topological polaritonics.
one_liner: Embedded gold nanodisk lattices with monolayer semiconductors reveal how interface quality governs strong coupling in plasmonic polariton devices.
why_it_matters: It identifies fabrication conditions needed to build robust large-area plasmonic polariton platforms for room-temperature photonic functionality.
detailed_summary: The work studies strong light-matter coupling between plasmonic gold nanodisk lattices and monolayer semiconductor excitons inside van der Waals heterostructures. By comparing opposite layer-order configurations, the authors show that strain and etch-related contamination strongly degrade exciton linewidth and therefore reduce coupling strength. Their embedding process minimizes interfacial disorder while maintaining plasmonic functionality in hBN-encapsulated monolayers. The resulting approach supports homogeneous large-area polariton lattices suited for more complex photonic band engineering.
platform_or_system: plasmonic gold nanodisk lattices with TMDC monolayers
main_result: The main result is that improved embedding and interface control significantly preserve exciton quality and enable stronger, more uniform plasmon-exciton coupling over large areas.
application_angle: This fabrication strategy is relevant to scalable polaritonic devices such as low-threshold light sources, polarization-control elements, and topological polariton platforms.

title: Programmable cavity-enhanced telecom quantum memory in thin-film lithium niobate
abstract: Spectrally multiplexed telecom quantum networks require quantum memories that combine efficient storage with programmable frequency addressing. An ideal integrated implementation should therefore unite a native telecom transition, efficient storage and fast on-chip spectral control. Here we demonstrate a cavity-enhanced quantum memory in an isotopically purified $^{167}\mathrm{Er}^{3+}$-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator. Long-lived hyperfine shelving states support persistent, high-contrast atomic frequency comb preparation, with a single-component comb lifetime of $277.6 \pm 52.6$s. Together with cavity impedance matching, this yields an on-chip storage efficiency of $23.3 \pm 0.5\%$ for 100-ns storage. The intrinsic electro-optic response of lithium niobate enables frequency-selective storage and routing of retrieved photons at rates up to 20~MHz with inter-channel crosstalk below $10^{-4}$. We further store and retrieve time-energy-entangled telecom photons, violating an entanglement-witness bound by more than 11 standard deviations and thus verifying the quantum nature of the storage process. Our results establish erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate as a programmable light--matter interface for spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.
one_liner: An erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring realizes programmable telecom quantum memory with cavity enhancement and low-crosstalk spectral routing.
why_it_matters: It combines native telecom storage, on-chip programmability, and entanglement preservation in a platform suited to multiplexed quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The device is an isotopically purified 167Er-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator operated as a cavity-enhanced atomic frequency comb memory. Long-lived hyperfine shelving enables persistent high-contrast comb preparation with a reported single-component comb lifetime of 277.6 s, while cavity impedance matching yields 23.3% on-chip storage efficiency for 100 ns storage. The electro-optic tunability of lithium niobate allows frequency-selective storage and routing of recalled photons at up to 20 MHz with inter-channel crosstalk below 10^-4. The memory also stores and retrieves time-energy-entangled telecom photons, surpassing an entanglement-witness bound by more than 11 standard deviations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a programmable cavity-enhanced telecom quantum memory with 23.3% storage efficiency, fast spectral addressing, and verified entanglement-preserving operation.
application_angle: This is a strong candidate building block for integrated spectrally multiplexed quantum repeaters and telecom-band network nodes with on-chip storage and routing.

title: The influence of strong coupling between single-photon source and spectral filter on photon statistics
abstract: One of the most common approaches for coupling optical single-photon sources and photonic integrated circuits is to use a cavity. The cavity acts as a spectral filter that distorts the light spectrum and changes its statistical properties. But in the general case one should take into account not only spectral filtering of light but also the spectral filter influence on the single-photon source dynamics. We build an effective analytical model for description of the cavity influence on the photon statistics of light emitted by the single-photon source as spectral filtering only. We show that this model correctly describes the photon statistics even in a strong-coupling regime between the single-photon source and the spectral filter. Our results can be useful for analytical modeling of photon statistics of quantum emitters strongly coupled to various electromagnetic interfaces.
one_liner: An analytical model shows when a cavity-filtered single-photon source can still be treated as spectral filtering, even under strong coupling.
why_it_matters: It simplifies prediction of emitted-photon statistics for emitter-interface stacks used in integrated quantum photonics without fully simulating joint dynamics.
detailed_summary: The paper studies how a cavity used as a spectral filter modifies both the spectrum and the counting statistics of a single-photon source. It derives an effective analytical model that represents the cavity influence as spectral filtering only, then tests that approximation in the strong-coupling regime between source and filter. The authors report that the reduced model still reproduces the photon statistics correctly, despite back-action on source dynamics being present in the full problem. This gives a compact route to analyze quantum-emitter interfaces coupled to resonant electromagnetic structures.
platform_or_system: single-photon source coupled to an optical cavity filter
main_result: An effective spectral-filtering model accurately captures emitted-photon statistics even when the source and spectral filter are strongly coupled.
application_angle: Useful for designing and analytically modeling cavity-coupled emitters in photonic integrated circuits and quantum-light interfaces.

title: Superconducting single-photon detectors for integrated quantum photonics
abstract: Single-photon detection possibility is a fundamental requirement for quantum technologies, including communication, computing and sensing. To achieve scalability and practical deployment, increasing attention is being directed toward integration of detectors with photonic integrated circuits, which offer compactness and compatibility with mass production. Superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors have emerged as the leading solution, combining near-unity efficiency, high temporal performance and the ability to be embedded across a wide range of photonic material platforms. In this review we trace the development of integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors from early demonstrations to recent advances, outlining the progress in device architectures, material engineering and integration strategies. We also discuss performance benchmarks, emerging alternative designs, the future opportunities and challenges for this rapidly evolving field.
one_liner: A review maps the state of integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors across photonic platforms, architectures, and performance tradeoffs.
why_it_matters: Integrated SNSPDs are a core enabling technology for scalable quantum photonics, communication, and sensing systems that need efficient on-chip photon readout.
detailed_summary: This review surveys the development of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors integrated with photonic integrated circuits. It covers detector architectures, material engineering, and heterogeneous integration across multiple photonic material platforms, with attention to benchmarks such as efficiency, timing performance, and scalability. The paper also discusses alternative detector designs and identifies remaining challenges for manufacturable, high-performance integration. As a synthesis paper, it is valuable as a technical map of the detector stack needed for deployable quantum photonic hardware.
platform_or_system: integrated superconducting nanowire detectors on photonic chips
main_result: The paper consolidates recent progress showing that integrated SNSPDs can deliver near-unity efficiency, excellent timing, and broad material-platform compatibility.
application_angle: Directly relevant to building compact, scalable on-chip receivers for quantum communication, sensing, and photonic quantum processors.

title: Efficient ultrafast homodyne detection of quantum light
abstract: Ultrafast continuous-variable quantum states offer new opportunities for advanced quantum technologies, but efficient homodyne detection of these states remains challenging. Here, we present a method for efficient ultrafast homodyne detection by exploiting temporal correlations in detector signals. By optimizing the temporal weight used to extract quadrature outcomes, we achieve a substantial increase in the signal-to-noise ratio of ultrafast homodyne detection, thereby improving the detection efficiency. We analyze the autocorrelations of shot noise and electronic noise and determine the optimal weight by solving a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem. The optimal weight enhances the squeezing and anti-squeezing levels observed experimentally. These results highlight the importance of optimized signal processing for efficient quantum measurements.
one_liner: Optimized temporal weighting of detector traces boosts signal-to-noise and effective efficiency in ultrafast homodyne measurements of quantum light.
why_it_matters: Better homodyne extraction improves readout of ultrafast continuous-variable states without changing the optical hardware, which is attractive for practical quantum measurement chains.
detailed_summary: The work targets ultrafast homodyne detection, where extracting quadrature values from short detector signals is limited by shot-noise and electronic-noise correlations. The authors analyze detector autocorrelations and determine the optimal temporal weighting function by solving a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem. Experimentally, this optimized weighting increases signal-to-noise ratio and raises the observed squeezing and anti-squeezing levels. The result is a measurement-side efficiency gain obtained through principled temporal mode processing.
main_result: Temporal-correlation-aware weighting improves quadrature extraction and measurably enhances the observed squeezing performance in ultrafast homodyne detection.
application_angle: Applicable to high-rate continuous-variable quantum optics and metrology systems where readout efficiency is limited by detector bandwidth and noise processing.

title: Nonlocal Topological Maxwell Demon Teleporting Ergotropy via Surface-Code Quantum Error Correction
abstract: We introduce a nonlocal Maxwell demon teleporting ergotropy at finite temperature via classical communication and a shared surface code. The teleported ergotropy is exponentially protected below a topological threshold. We identify a thermodynamic phase transition separating a profitable demon phase from a thermal phase. A quadratic infrastructure cost strictly enforces the second law, imposing a fundamental thermodynamic horizon on separation distance. This establishes quantum error correction as a resource for nonlocal thermodynamics beyond fault-tolerant computation.
one_liner: A surface-code-based Maxwell demon protocol is proposed to teleport ergotropy nonlocally with exponential topological protection below threshold.
why_it_matters: It connects quantum error correction to thermodynamic resource transport, suggesting protected nonlocal energy-control primitives beyond standard fault-tolerant computing.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a nonlocal Maxwell demon that teleports ergotropy at finite temperature using classical communication and a shared surface code. In the proposed model, the teleported ergotropy is exponentially protected when operating below a topological threshold, and the authors identify a phase transition between a profitable demon regime and a thermal regime. They further argue that a quadratic infrastructure cost preserves the second law by imposing a distance-dependent thermodynamic horizon. The work frames quantum error correction as an operational thermodynamic resource, not only a computational one.
main_result: The authors show theoretically that surface-code resources can support exponentially protected nonlocal ergotropy transfer below threshold, with a distinct thermodynamic phase boundary.
application_angle: Relevant to future fault-tolerant hardware concepts where error-corrected quantum resources may also support robust thermodynamic or energy-management tasks.

title: Quantum-Secure Physical Unclonable Function enabled by Silicon Photonics Integrated Circuits
abstract: Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) are hardware security primitives whose inherent physical complexity can be exploited for secure authentication and cryptographic key generation. Silicon photonic devices, owing to their suitability for quantum and artificial intelligence applications alongside standard CMOS fabrication processes, constitute a highly promising substrate for integrated multifunctional PUFs. Despite the advanced security guarantees offered by quantum cryptographic protocols and the central role of silicon photonics in quantum technologies, quantum readout strategies based on single-photon states for photonic PUFs remain largely unexplored. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a silicon nitride (SiN) programmable photonic Mach Zehnder interferometer mesh that implements a unitary transformation and operates as a PUF, whose secret physical signature arises from uncontrollable waveguide variations during fabrication. Using experimentally derived parameters from the SiN integrated mesh, we further introduce and numerically evaluate a quantum readout protocol that combines single-photon states with PUFs. Maximally mixed quantum states are employed to conceal the underlying unitary transformation from passive eavesdropping. Security against adversaries possessing devices fabricated under similar conditions is assessed, with authentication performance quantified through Monte Carlo analysis of the false acceptance and false rejection rates as a function of the number of detected events and corrected errors. The results indicate exceptional performance with equal error rates as low as 10 to the minus 14, highlighting the potential of quantum secure PUFs for high security authentication applications.
one_liner: A silicon-photonic PUF with single-photon quantum readout achieves extremely low simulated authentication error rates for hardware security.
why_it_matters: It brings quantum-secure authentication to CMOS-compatible photonic hardware, combining unclonable fabrication disorder with quantum readout concealment.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally demonstrate a programmable silicon nitride Mach-Zehnder interferometer mesh acting as a physical unclonable function whose secret signature derives from fabrication-induced waveguide variations. Using parameters extracted from the device, they then numerically study a quantum readout protocol based on single-photon states and maximally mixed inputs to hide the implemented unitary from passive eavesdroppers. Monte Carlo analysis evaluates false acceptance and false rejection versus detected events and corrected errors, yielding equal error rates down to 10^-14. The work combines integrated photonics, disorder-based identity, and quantum-state-based interrogation into a strong authentication primitive.
platform_or_system: silicon nitride programmable MZI mesh PUF
main_result: A SiN photonic PUF plus quantum single-photon readout protocol is shown to support authentication with simulated equal error rates as low as 10^-14.
application_angle: Promising for secure photonic authentication modules and quantum-aware hardware security in communication and edge devices built on CMOS-compatible platforms.

title: Accurate Modeling of Rydberg Atoms and Their Interactions: Theory and Implementation in PairInteraction
abstract: Rydberg atoms provide a powerful platform for exploring strongly interacting quantum systems, both in free space and in structured electromagnetic environments, with growing applications in quantum technology. Accurately modeling their single-atom properties and mutual interactions is essential for interpreting experiments and designing new architectures. We present a unified theoretical framework for Rydberg atoms and their interactions based on multi-channel quantum defect theory (MQDT) and static electromagnetic Green's tensors. MQDT provides a precise description of Rydberg states of divalent atoms such as strontium and ytterbium, while the Green's tensor formalism provides a general and flexible approach for calculating interactions between two Rydberg atoms in arbitrary geometries, including modifications induced by nearby surfaces. We implement this framework in an updated version of the open-source PairInteraction software [Weber et al., J.~Phys.~B~50 (2017)]. The implementation leverages high-performance libraries and achieves speedups of one order of magnitude for pair-potential calculations compared to prior software. We demonstrate the capabilities of the framework through example applications to divalent atoms and show excellent agreement with experimental data for an exemplary Stark map of $^{174}$Yb. The modular software architecture enables the community to extend it further.
one_liner: An upgraded PairInteraction framework delivers faster, more accurate modeling of Rydberg atoms and surface-modified interactions in complex geometries.
why_it_matters: Accurate and faster simulation tools are essential for designing Rydberg-based quantum devices, links, and atom-surface architectures with realistic interaction models.
detailed_summary: This paper presents a unified theory for Rydberg atoms that combines multi-channel quantum defect theory for precise single-atom structure with static electromagnetic Green's tensors for two-atom interactions in arbitrary environments. The framework handles divalent species such as strontium and ytterbium and includes interaction modifications from nearby surfaces or structured electromagnetic settings. Implemented in an updated open-source PairInteraction package, it achieves roughly an order-of-magnitude speedup for pair-potential calculations and matches experimental data well, including a Stark map of 174Yb. The modular software stack is intended as a practical design and interpretation tool for the community.
main_result: The updated PairInteraction implementation unifies accurate Rydberg-state and interaction modeling while providing about 10x faster pair-potential calculations with strong experimental agreement.
application_angle: Supports realistic design of Rydberg quantum hardware and mediated-interaction architectures, including devices affected by nearby surfaces or structured fields.

title: Majorana Constellations: A Geometric Lens on Multipartite Entanglement and Geometric Phases
abstract: The Majorana stellar representation translates abstract quantum spin states into intuitive geometric constellations on the Bloch sphere, revealing symmetries, degeneracies, and correlations that traditional algebraic methods often obscure. Within quantum information science, this framework provides a powerful lens for characterizing symmetric multi-qubit and higher-spin systems. By encoding entanglement directly into spatial coordinates, the constellation geometry yields exact measures of concurrence, three-tangle, and genuine multipartite entanglement, while its dynamical evolution uncovers internal anomalous contributions to geometric phases. While interest in stellar representations has resurged, existing literature remains fragmented, lacking a unified treatment of these entanglement-specific metrics and their higher-dimensional dynamics. This review synthesizes the entanglement-centric perspective on Majorana representations, bridging discrete algebraic classifications (e.g., SLOCC orbits) with continuous geometric interpretations. Crucially, we highlight how this framework circumvents \#P-hard computational bottlenecks, leveraging polynomial-time tractability to evaluate multipartite invariants. We detail the interplay between constellation topology and higher-spin Berry/Hannay phases, explore extensions beyond pure symmetric states, and review applications in quantum metrology, state engineering, and condensed-matter physics. By foregrounding entanglement as the unifying theme, this comprehensive examination establishes Majorana stars as a fundamental geometric language, uniquely positioned to inspire new theoretical and experimental directions in quantum technologies.
one_liner: Review of Majorana stellar constellations as a geometric toolkit for multipartite entanglement, phases, and metrology.
why_it_matters: It links entanglement structure to tractable geometric objects, potentially simplifying characterization and control in symmetric quantum sensors and spin platforms.
detailed_summary: This review organizes the Majorana stellar representation as a unified geometric language for symmetric multi-qubit and higher-spin states. It explains how constellation features encode concurrence, three-tangle, and genuine multipartite entanglement, while also exposing anomalous internal contributions to geometric phases. A key technical point is that relevant multipartite invariants can be evaluated in polynomial time within this framework, avoiding some otherwise intractable algebraic bottlenecks. The paper also surveys extensions beyond pure symmetric states and discusses implications for metrology, state engineering, and condensed-matter settings.
main_result: The paper synthesizes a tractable geometric framework that connects Majorana constellations to multipartite entanglement measures and higher-spin geometric phases.
application_angle: The framework could support practical state certification and design in spin-based quantum sensors and entanglement-enhanced metrology protocols.

title: A Resource-Driven Framework for Configurable Entanglement in Quantum Networks
abstract: Shared multipartite entanglement defines a ``whatever channel'', i.e., a latent communication substrate that does not determine a priori which end-to-end entangled links are activated, but can be configured to support different entanglement-connectivity graphs through Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC). Building on this, we propose a resource-driven framework in which multipartite entanglement is treated as a programmable resource that induces a space of admissible entanglement-graph configurations. Within this framework, connectivity provisioning emerges as a particular instance of a more general resource reconfiguration process. To support this paradigm, we introduce a set of structural design parameters that characterize the operational degrees of freedom of the resource and define the admissible transformations independently of the specific mechanism used to realize them. We then formalize Entanglement Rolling as a measurement-based protocol that operates over the induced configuration space, enabling the systematic reconfiguration of the shared resource across a family of multipartite states. Finally, we analyze the proposed framework under realistic noise conditions. Leveraging the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF), we derive closed-form noise maps that characterize the effect of noise on the resource transformations and show that the proposed approach maintains reliable performance under relevant noise processes.
one_liner: Introduces a configurable-network model where multipartite entanglement is reconfigured into different end-to-end link graphs via LOCC.
why_it_matters: It reframes shared entanglement as a programmable network resource and provides a noise-aware protocol for adaptive quantum connectivity provisioning.
